contested.clean_text: {{Infobox music genre | name = Manele | stylistic_origins = | cultural_origins = Late 18th century, Ottoman Romania | derivatives = | subgenres = | fusiongenres = | regional_scenes = | local_scenes = | other_topics = }} Manele (from Romanian, fem. sg. manea; pl. manele, the plural form being more common) is a genre of pop folk music from Romania. The manele can be divided into "classical manele" and "modern manele". The "classical manele" are a Turkish-derived genre performed by Romani musicians called lăutari in a lăutărească manner, In the 1960s a type of lăutarească manea appeared, by adding texts to the geampara, a type of lăutaresc genre of Turkish origin. The modern manele originated in the 1980s and early 1990s as translations and imitations of Turkish and Arabic songs. A well known Romanian manele singer, Adrian Copilul Minune traces it to a genre known as "turceasca" (Turkish). The genre has been rocked by accusations of plagiarism a number of times, with manele singers adapting popular songs from Greece, Bulgaria, Serbia and Turkey, without giving due credit. The accusations increased especially after the hit "De ce mă minți" ("Why are you lying to me?") proved to be a mere cover of Despina Vandi's song "M'agapas"/"Ah kardoula mou". Further plagiarism accusations surrounded a well known manele singer's track called "Supărat" ("Upset") which was proven by third parties to be plagiarized from a Croatian song (Umoran by Jasmin Stavros). Although this song was not technically a manea, it furthered the controversy surrounding this music genre and Romania's image. Most radio and television channels or media boycott manele music. thumb|The difference between old and modern (contemporary) manea rhythmic pattern Manele is a mixture of "oriental" Romanian folk and contemporary pop music with bases on Balkan influences The lyrics usually refer to themes of love, enemies, money, alcoholism and difficulties of life in general, or some of the songs are specifically dedicated to parties, weddings, funerals, etc. Manele style contains objections to music primitiveness and low performance. Manele are widely criticized for their lyrical content, which often consists of boasts about the singer's supposed sex appeal, intellect, wealth, social status, and superiority over so-called "enemies". Many singers use bad grammar, repetitive and simplistic rhymes suitable for chanting and are sometimes vulgar and/or misogynistic. Singers sometimes make trilling or yelling sounds during instrumental parts of their songs, an aspect that has been parodied many times. Some manele also have music videos, of which many are of poor quality while others are recorded in nightclubs or during private parties. Manele composers and players also use the term "oriental music" or "party music" for their creation, and consider their music a sub-genre of traditional, folk Roma music. Traditional Roma music is usually played on classical instruments by a live band (taraf) of lăutari and has classical lyrics, while manele is usually sung by only one performer using modern instruments (generally synthesizers) as backup. Most manele are recorded in small recording studios, owned by the singer himself or by a group of singers, since major recording labels refuse to contract them. However, there are some exceptions: for example, Stana Izbașa and Nicu Paleru sing live, often with traditional instruments. The word "manea" is of Turkish origin: mâni is a form of Turkish folk song, in form of quatrains. The word "manea" is the singular form and it refers to the musical piece itself, as belonging to Manele genre. The accent is on the second syllable: maneá. The plural version, more commonly used, manele, refers to: *the musical genre (e.g.: "I'm listening to manele.", in Romanian: "Ascult Manele.") *two or more manea songs (e.g.: "What are the latest manele (songs) you know?", in Romanian: "Care sunt cele mai noi manele pe care le știi?) The adjective form of the word is manelist, which is sometimes used in Romanian with a pejorative figurative form. Manelists have created a distinct image on the Romanian music scene, by showing their own fashion style. Many of the manelists use luxurious and casual, even styles combined altogether to form the specific manele fashion. Typical manele apparel includes flashy jewelry and affordable luxury clothing brands (such as Versace, Armani or Dolce & Gabbana) or certain sport brands (especially Nike). Such brands are an important part of manele culture, and they are even featured sometimes in lyrics. Manele are a strongly disputed genre in Romania, with many representatives of Romanian upper-middle and intellectual class opposing this "musical" movement (and its popularization) mostly because of its usage of faulty grammar, overly simplistic or childish lyrics and subject matter and/or encouragement of demeaning behaviours towards other people, as well as an antisocial overall message. The fact that manele lyrics are considered by many to be rude and of poor taste, coupled with widespread racism against Romani, who account for the bulk of manele performers, has led to increasing hostility between fans and opponents. C. Tepercea, a National Audio-visual Board member who did a study on the genre for the board considered it "the genre for the mentally challenged" in an interview. Even proposals to ban this type of music have been voiced. Romanian-American professor Cezar Giosan further compares the genre in an article in Dilema Veche with the early stages of rock-and-roll (and Elvis), early rap and reggaeton, music starting out from the outcast classes of society, being shunned by the higher classes for the simple reason of its origin, only to explode into mainstream later on. The same professor considers the genre as being a form of originality coming from below, with the singers having a genuine (albeit rough and uneducated) talent in music, with the lyrics being just a reflection of basic, simple human needs. Both consider that manele is a valuable representation of Romanian popular culture, and would like it encouraged. Famous Romani-Romanian violin player Florin Niculescu said that manele singers are talented, but lack musical education. On Romanian television stations, manele performers and music are particularly seen on specialized manele television stations, such as Taraf TV or Manele TV. While mainstream radio stations do not air manele, a lot of smaller stations do, especially in Romania's capital, Bucharest. Occasionally, manele interpreters appear on New Year's Eve programs on television stations. Manele have been prohibited in some cities of Romania in public transport, taxis or festivals. * Brothers Gore (Aurel and Victor Gore) - instrumental manele * Gabi Luncă * Romica Puceanu * Azur (vocalist: Nelu Vlad) - the first band to use electronic beats * Albatros (vocalist: Iolanda Cristea a.k.a. Naste din Berceni) * Generic (vocalist: Dan Ciotoi) * Miracol C (vocalist: Cezar Duțu a.k.a. Cezarică) * Odeon (vocalist: Costel Geambașu) * Dan Armeanca - considered the godfather of Romani pop * Adrian Minune (previously Adrian Copilul Minune, Adi de Vito) * Costi Ioniță * Carmen Șerban Popular performers include Florin Salam, Nicolae Guță, Vali Vijelie, Sandu Ciorba, Tzanca Uraganu, Costel Biju, and DeSanto. * Chalga, in Bulgaria * Laïko, in Greece * Turbo-folk, in Serbia * Music of Lebanon, in Lebanon * Arabic pop music, in the Arab world * [https://web.archive.org/web/20171005202314/http://www.muzeultaranuluiroman.ro/conferinte/?p=116 The notes of a conference on manele at the "National Peasant Museum"] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100706033355/http://www.divers.ro/focus_ro?wid=37452&func=viewSubmission&sid=4670 Interview with ethnomusicologist Speranţa Rădulescu] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20191105121038/https://www.maneleradio.net/ Radio Manele] * [https://web.archive.org/web/20230512044453/https://www.theregister.com/2005/04/28/manele-hating_malware VXer targets Romanian gypsy music] Category:Music of Romania Category:Pop music genres Category:Balkan music Category:Turkish words and phrases Category:Romani music

It is a redirect to [[Fictional African countries#S|sub-section S under Fictional Africal countries]]. I think this is appropriate. [[User:Julianhall|Julianhall]] ([[User talk:Julianhall|talk]]) 11:39, 3 December 2008 (UTC) ==Date the Movie is set== It said on here that the movie is set in they year 2017, after reading the article that this related too it said that if anyone works out the number of president involved in the series to date it has to be set in 2017. This is wrong, so i removed it. A short explanation is below. Day One aired in 2001. If we say that it was based in 2000 and David Palmers presidential camapign was for the 2000 elections we can deduce that david palmer was inagurated on 1st January 2001. Day Two David palmer still president. The series took place 18 months after the events of day one. Circa 2001. Day three took palce three years after the events of day two. David Palmer is running to gain nomination for the presidential race so this puts the series arround 2004. Day four takes place 18 months after the events of day three. John Keeler is now president after beating Palmer for the party nomination, in this series he his mortally injursed during a terrorist attack on air force one and charles logan is sworn in under the 28th ammendment. circa 2005/early 2006 Day five takes place another 18 months after Day four Charles Logan is still president and removed from office at the end of the series by Jack Bauer, Hal Gardner the VP of Logan is assumed to ascend to the presidency. circa 2008. Day Six the presidential term of Hal Gardner came to a end shortly before and Wayne Palmer the brother of David Palmer is now president. Due to being sevearly injured during a terrorist attack his VP Noah Daniels takes control. circa 2009. 24: Redemption and Day 7. Allison Taylor is inuagurated making this time arround 2012 elections and January 2013 inaguration day. [[Special:Contributions/90.195.56.51|90.195.56.51]] ([[User talk:90.195.56.51|talk]]) 17:11, 23 July 2008 (UTC) Jon Wilson The day is January 20, 2013, 3 PM to 5 PM. The presidential inauguration is at 12 noon Washington DC Time, and President Taylor was inaugurated at the end of the episode. I guess it isn't exactly in real time. [[User:Mr. moose|Mr. moose]] ([[User talk:Mr. moose|talk]]) 04:32, 25 November 2008 (UTC) ([[Special:Contributions/79.69.179.156|79.69.179.156]] ([[User talk:79.69.179.156|talk]]) 20:50, 16 July 2008 (UTC)) :While I appreciate your statement, it constitutes [[WP:OR|original research]], which cannot be used when creating article content. Please note that continually reverting or removing the same content can constitute an [[WP:EW|edit war]]. Hope this helps, '''''[[User:Gazimoff|Gazi]][[User talk:Gazimoff|moff]]'''''mentor]]/[[Wikipedia:Editor review/Gazimoff|review]]) 17:32, 23 July 2008 (UTC) ::Forgive me if i am wrong but this is based on a TV series no? whats more reliable source than the content thats on the series? [[Special:Contributions/90.195.56.51|90.195.56.51]] ([[User talk:90.195.56.51|talk]]) 22:48, 23 July 2008 (UTC) Jon Wilson *What you've said constitutes original research, you have basically gone and calculated, by yourself, the rough year that the series is set in. Reliable sources are better to be used, ans third party sources should always be used over a primary source. Please have a look at the source, and see the comment regarding the year that this is set in. Thanks, [[User:Steve Crossin|Steve Crossin]] [[User:Steve Crossin/Contact|(contact)]] 01:02, 24 July 2008 (UTC) == Title Changed == I just wanted to note that the prequel title has been changed from Exile to 24: Redemption. Source: http://www.tvshowsondvd.com/news/24-Exile-Renamed/10460 [[Special:Contributions/24.109.235.79|24.109.235.79]] ([[User talk:24.109.235.79|talk]]) 14:06, 4 September 2008 (UTC) :{{done}} '''''ChimpanzeeUK''''' - [[User:ChimpanzeeUK|User]] | [[User talk:ChimpanzeeUK|Talk]] | [[Special:Contributions/ChimpanzeeUK|Contribs]] 18:26, 4 September 2008 (UTC) Hi Can anyone tell me a site where to find the first act (17 minutes) of 24: Season 7 that is on the 24: Redemption DVD. I can't get the DVD because I'm in New Zealand, but I want to see the first act of Season 7. Does anybody know of a site where it has been uploaded? I've tried torrent sites and video streaming sites but no luck. Please, somebody, help me out. Cheers —Preceding [[Wikipedia:Signatures|unsigned]] comment added by [[User:Caradone|Caradone]] ([[User talk:Caradone|talk]] • [[Special:Contributions/Caradone|contribs]]) 21:31, 25 November 2008 (UTC) == GA pre-review == I don't have time to do a formal review for this article, but in order to pass one in the future, this article is going to need a reception section with critical commentary, viewier numbers, and a citation for the citation needed tag. [[User:Nikki311|'''Nikki''']]'''♥'''[[User Talk:Nikki311|'''311''']] 17:23, 5 December 2008 (UTC) == GA review == This article is nowhere near the length or comprehensiveness that is expected of a good article: the production section is short and there are no critical reviews at all. For a TV movie that gets 12 million viewers, I find it hard to believe. The prose can be improved vastly as well; [http://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=24%3A_Redemption&diff=cur&oldid=prev this] was done with little effort and already looks better. '''[[User:Sceptre|Sceptre]]''' ([[User talk:Sceptre|talk]]) 23:12, 29 December 2008 (UTC) == Football to Soccer == I guess, as '24' is an American show, it should be 'soccer', despite being described as 'football' in the movie. Also, I would like to tell Matthew R Dunn that I am not a Brit, but am an American who agrees with the term football for 'soccer' and American football for 'football'. [[Special:Contributions/76.111.67.200|76.111.67.200]] ([[User talk:76.111.67.200|talk]]) 01:59, 14 January 2009 (UTC) :Actually, if you may have misintrupret what I said in the edit summary, I was actually referring to myself as a Brit (as I usually twists some words now and then). Anyway, this morning, I reverted a similar edit, and he left me a message on my talk page, so now I'm starting to get a little confused about what to do. So I'm open to suggestions about what to do concerning football/soccer. -- [[User:Matthew R Dunn|Matthew R Dunn]] ([[User talk:Matthew R Dunn|talk]]) 02:35, 14 January 2009 (UTC) :: Another Brit here. For absolute clarity, i would suggest use of 'Soccer', 'American Football', etc. 'Football' is an ambiguous term, and could potentially be used to refer to any of the codes of football (American, Canadian, Australian Rules, Association, Rugby, Gaelic, etc.). I am strongly against use of the term 'football', other than when used to refer to all of the various sports that fall under the umbrella term 'football', as defined [[Football|here]]. [[User:Julianhall|Julianhall]] ([[User talk:Julianhall|talk]]) 11:55, 14 January 2009 (UTC) :::Alright --[[Special:Contributions/76.111.67.200|76.111.67.200]] ([[User talk:76.111.67.200|talk]]) 23:34, 14 January 2009 (UTC) == Not a "prequel" == I deleted the word "prequel" in the article. It may have been called a prequel in publicity, but regardless it is not. A "prequel" goes back in the timeline. (If it had been shown AFTER day 7 was broadcast, it would be a prequel.) Redemption occurs after Day 6, before Day 7, so it is just in the normal timeline. (24 never has flashbacks, except recaps/previouslies, after all.) Call it an introduction, prelude, but not a "prequel". See [[prequel]] before you argue with this. ("A prequel is a work that portrays events and/or aspects of a '''previously completed narrative''', but is set prior to the existing narrative.") [[User:Barsoomian|Barsoomian]] ([[User talk:Barsoomian|talk]]) 06:38, 19 January 2009 (UTC) == Acute accent marks on Colonel Dubaku's first name == I just noticed that all of the acute accent marks were removed from 'Iké'. Shouldn't they have acute accent marks on them? It's to mark how his name is pronounced (especially as a name most likely French-influenced), and it is also how the name is spelled (with the accent mark) when it is introduced with the character the name is of at the beginning of each episode, during the recap of the previous episode. [[Special:Contributions/76.111.67.200|76.111.67.200]] ([[User talk:76.111.67.200|talk]]) 03:39, 20 January 2009 (UTC) : Yes, it's "Iké", as seen on the chyrons. Fixed that. [[User:Barsoomian|Barsoomian]] ([[User talk:Barsoomian|talk]]) 00:32, 17 February 2009 (UTC) == Extended Edition Runtime == I've changed the extended edition runtime from 120 to 102 minutes. Various sources like IMDB state that the USA extended edition is 102 minutes, not 120. [[Special:Contributions/85.147.240.236|85.147.240.236]] ([[User talk:85.147.240.236|talk]]) 15:59, 1 April 2009 (UTC) == Dead link == During several automated bot runs the following external link was found to be unavailable. Please check if the link is in fact down and fix or remove it in that case! * http://www.hollywoodreporter.com/hr/content_display/television/news/e3id08e9f14f520f15d010fb0389d8bbeb3 ** In [[Eric Lively]] on 2011-03-18 07:41:57, 404 Not Found ** In [[Gil Bellows]] on 2011-03-22 05:50:47, 404 Not Found ** In [[24: Redemption]] on 2011-06-19 05:35:54, 404 Not Found --[[User:JeffGBot|JeffGBot]] ([[User talk:JeffGBot|talk]]) 05:36, 19 June 2011 (UTC) {{Talk:24: Redemption/GA1}} == External links modified == Hello fellow Wikipedians, I have just modified {{plural:3|one external link|3 external links}} on [[24: Redemption]]. This is wrong, so i removed it. A short explanation is below. Day One aired in 2001. If we say that it was based in 2000 and David Palmers presidential camapign was for the 2000 elections we can deduce that david palmer was inagurated on 1st January 2001. Day Two David palmer still president. The series took place 18 months after the events of day one. Circa 2001. Day three took palce three years after the events of day two. David Palmer is running to gain nomination for the presidential race so this puts the series arround 2004. Day four takes place 18 months after the events of day three. John Keeler is now president after beating Palmer for the party nomination, in this series he his mortally injursed during a terrorist attack on air force one and charles logan is sworn in under the 28th ammendment. circa 2005/early 2006 Day five takes place another 18 months after Day four Charles Logan is still president and removed from office at the end of the series by Jack Bauer, Hal Gardner the VP of Logan is assumed to ascend to the presidency. circa 2008. Day Six the presidential term of Hal Gardner came to a end shortly before and Wayne Palmer the brother of David Palmer is now president. Due to being sevearly injured during a terrorist attack his VP Noah Daniels takes control. circa 2009. 24: Redemption and Day 7. Allison Taylor is inuagurated making this time arround 2012 elections and January 2013 inaguration day. 90.195.56.51 (talk) 17:11, 23 July 2008 (UTC) Jon Wilson The day is January 20, 2013, 3 PM to 5 PM. Sceptre (talk) 23:12, 29 December 2008 (UTC) I guess, as '24' is an American show, it should be 'soccer', despite being described as 'football' in the movie. Also, I would like to tell Matthew R Dunn that I am not a Brit, but am an American who agrees with the term football for 'soccer' and American football for 'football'. 76.111.67.200 (talk) 01:59, 14 January 2009 (UTC) :Actually, if you may have misintrupret what I said in the edit summary, I was actually referring to myself as a Brit (as I usually twists some words now and then). Anyway, this morning, I reverted a similar edit, and he left me a message on my talk page, so now I'm starting to get a little confused about what to do. So I'm open to suggestions about what to do concerning football/soccer. -- Matthew R Dunn (talk) 02:35, 14 January 2009 (UTC) :: Another Brit here. For absolute clarity, i would suggest use of 'Soccer', 'American Football', etc. 'Football' is an ambiguous term, and could potentially be used to refer to any of the codes of football (American, Canadian, Australian Rules, Association, Rugby, Gaelic, etc.). I am strongly against use of the term 'football', other than when used to refer to all of the various sports that fall under the umbrella term 'football', as defined here. Julianhall (talk) 11:55, 14 January 2009 (UTC) :::Alright --76.111.67.200 (talk) 23:34, 14 January 2009 (UTC) I deleted the word "prequel" in the article. It may have been called a prequel in publicity, but regardless it is not. A "prequel" goes back in the timeline. (If it had been shown AFTER day 7 was broadcast, it would be a prequel.) Redemption occurs after Day 6, before Day 7, so it is just in the normal timeline. (24 never has flashbacks, except recaps/previouslies, after all.) Call it an introduction, prelude, but not a "prequel". See prequel before you argue with this. ("A prequel is a work that portrays events and/or aspects of a previously completed narrative, but is set prior to the existing narrative.") contested.title: Lewis Namier
contested.raw_text:
{{Short description|British historian (1888–1960)}}
{{EngvarB|date=August 2014}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=August 2014}}
[[Image:SirLewisBernsteinNamier.jpg|thumb|right|200px|{{center|Namier in 1915}}]]

'''Sir Lewis Bernstein Namier''' ({{IPAc-en|ˈ|n|eɪ|m|i|ər}};<ref>{{cite dictionary|title=Definition of 'Namier' |url=http://www.collinsdictionary.com/dictionary/english/namier|dictionary=[[Collins English Dictionary|collinsdictionary.com]]|access-date=29 September 2013}}</ref> 27 June 1888 – 19 August 1960) was a British historian of [[Polish-Jewish]] background. His best-known works were ''[[The Structure of Politics at the Accession of George III]]'' (1929), ''England in the Age of the American Revolution'' (1930) and the ''[[History of Parliament]]'' series (begun 1940) he edited later in his life with [[John Brooke (British historian)|John Brooke]].

== Life ==
{{More citations needed|section|date=January 2023}}
Namier was born '''Ludwik Bernstein Niemirowski''' in [[Wola Okrzejska]] in the [[Partitions of Poland|Russian-controlled]] [[Congress Poland]], now part of the [[Lublin Voivodeship]] of southeastern Poland, although his family moved to [[Austrian Poland]] in 1890.<ref name="Colley 1989 x">{{Harvnb|Colley|1989|p=x}}</ref> His family were secular-minded Polish-Jewish gentry. His father, with whom young Lewis often quarreled, idolized the [[Austro-Hungarian Empire]]. By contrast, Namier throughout his life detested it. Before coming to England he was educated at the [[University of Lviv|University of Lwów]] in [[Austrian Galicia]] (now in [[Ukraine]]) and the [[University of Lausanne]]. At Lausanne, Namier heard [[Vilfredo Pareto]] lecture, and Pareto's ideas about elites would have a great influence on his thinking.

Namier emigrated to the United Kingdom in 1907,<ref name="Colley 1989 9">{{Harvnb|Colley|1989|p=9}}</ref> studied first at the [[London School of Economics]] for a year and then at [[Balliol College]], [[University of Oxford|Oxford]], from 1908,<ref>{{Harvnb|Cairns|1974|p=11}}</ref> and became a [[British subject]] in 1913, whereupon he [[Anglicization|anglicised]] his name.<ref name="Colley 1989 9"/>

During the [[First World War]], he fought as a private with the 20th [[Royal Fusiliers]] in 1914–15 but was discharged owing to poor eyesight. He then held positions with the Propaganda Department (1915–17), the Department of Information (1917–18) and finally with the [[Political Intelligence Department (1918–1920)|Political Intelligence Department]] of the Foreign Office (1918–20).

===Professional career===
Following the defeat of Germany in World War One, Namier joined the British delegation at the [[Paris Peace Conference, 1919|Versailles Peace Conference]] of 1919. Based on his personal background, Namier maintained a firm personal interest in Russian affairs; however, he was also seen as one of the biggest enemies of the newly-independent Polish state in the British political environment. During the [[Polish–Soviet War]] his relationship with the Polish delegation was highly antagonistic and his attitude towards Poland and Polish territories openly hostile. Namier was later accused of changing the British proposal – the "[[Curzon Line]]" – for the eastern border of Poland by leaving the city of [[Lviv]] (in Polish, Lwów) and the Oil Basin on the eastern side when the British Foreign Office sent a cable to the Commissar for Foreign Affairs of Bolshevik Russia, {{Interlanguage link|Georgy Chicherin{{!}}Georgy Vasilyevich Chicherin|ru|3=Чичерин, Георгий Васильевич}}.<ref name="Eberhardt"/>

The Polish delegation had no knowledge of the existence of Line "A" whatsoever since the idea of handing Lwów over to the Bolsheviks was rejected by Prime Minister [[Władysław Grabski]] at the very beginning of talks. Lwów had never been under the rule of Moscow in its history.<ref name="BR">{{cite journal |title=Lewis Namier, the Curzon Line, and the shaping of Poland's eastern frontier after World War I |author=Bartłomiej Rusin |publisher=Jagellonian University |journal=Studies into the History of Central-Eastern Europe and Russia |volume=XLVIII |url=http://journals.indexcopernicus.com/fulltxt.php?ICID=1127276 |at=Section 1: 20 (16 / 22) in PDF |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151222102914/http://journals.indexcopernicus.com/fulltxt.php?ICID=1127276 |archive-date=22 December 2015 }}</ref>

Professor [[Piotr Eberhardt]] from the [[Polish Academy of Sciences]] speculates that [[Lloyd George]] could have been aware of Namier's modification;<ref name="Eberhardt"/> Bartłomiej Rusin refutes the claims that Namier was responsible and calls him "merely a convenient supplier of anti‑Polish arguments".<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Rusin|first1=Bartłomiej|title=Lewis Namier, the Curzon Line, and the shaping of Poland's eastern frontier after World War I|journal=Studia z Dziejów Rosji i Europy Środkowo-Wschodniej|date=2014|volume=48|issue=1|pages=95–116|doi=10.12775/SDR.2013.05|url=http://www.sdr-ihpan.edu.pl/images/2013-tom-48/WA303_59317_A453-SzDR-R-48_Rusin.pdf|access-date=21 August 2017|doi-access=free}}</ref>

The earlier-approved compromised version of the Curzon Line which was approved at the [[Spa Conference of 1920|Spa Conference]] in Belgium was renamed by Namier as Curzon Line "B".<ref>{{Harvnb|Davies|1971}}.</ref> Chicherin relayed this document to Lenin who rejected it nevertheless, assured of his victory over Poland followed by a planned annexation of its entire territory.<ref name="Eberhardt">{{cite journal |title=The Curzon Line as the eastern boundary of Poland: the origins and the political background |journal=[[Geographia Polonica]] |year=2012 |volume=85, 1, pp. 5-21 |publisher=[[Polish Academy of Sciences]] Institute of Spatial Organization |at=8-9 / 18 in PDF |url=http://rcin.org.pl/Content/28362/WA51_46563_r2012-t85-no1_G-Polonica-Eberhardt.pdf |via=direct download, 1.27 MB |last=Eberhardt |first=Piotr }}</ref>

In one of his memoranda Namier falsified the results of a national census from Eastern Galicia originating from [[Austrian Partition|Austria-Hungary]]. He single-handedly reduced the number of ethnic Poles living in the region from 2 million down to 600–700 inhabitants. Professor [[Anna M. Cienciala]] believes that Namier was not the original initiator of this misrepresentation, but merely an unscrupulous supplier of handy arguments for the anti-Polish lobby among the Entente members.<ref name="BR2">{{cite journal |title=Lewis Namier, the Curzon Line, and the shaping of Poland's eastern frontier... |author=Bartłomiej Rusin |publisher=Ibidem |url=http://journals.indexcopernicus.com/fulltxt.php?ICID=1127276 |at=Section 1: 13 (9-10 / 22) in PDF |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151222102914/http://journals.indexcopernicus.com/fulltxt.php?ICID=1127276 |archive-date=22 December 2015  }}</ref>

{{POV|date=July 2024}}

After leaving government service, Namier taught at [[Balliol College, Oxford|Balliol]] (1920–21) before going into business for himself. Later Namier, who was a long-time [[Zionism|Zionist]], worked as political secretary for the [[Jewish Agency]] in Palestine (1929–31). For a time he was a close friend and associate of [[Chaim Weizmann]], but Weizmann later severed relations with Namier when the latter converted to [[Anglicanism]] to marry his second wife.

Namier served as professor at the [[University of Manchester]] from 1931 until his retirement in 1953, having been loudly cheered by his students at the conclusion of his last lecture there on European History. Namier remained active in various Zionist groups (in particular, lobbying the British government to allow the creation of what he called a Jewish Fighting Force in the [[Mandatory Palestine|Mandate of Palestine]]) and from 1933 was engaged in efforts on behalf of Jewish refugees from Germany.

He was married twice and knighted in 1952 at the onset of [[Cold War]]. Also in 1952, Namier was given the honour of delivering the [[Romanes Lecture]], on which subject Namier chose ''Monarchy and the Party System''.

== Personal life ==
In 1917, Namier married a Russian refugee, Clara Sophia Edeleff-Poniatowska, although they separated in 1921.{{cn|date=June 2025}} He was by this time estranged from his father so that when his father died in 1922 the family estate was left to Namier's sister.{{cn|date=June 2025}} In 1940 when he feared a Nazi invasion of Britain he burned a large number of his papers and arranged to commit suicide.{{cn|date=June 2025}}

In 1945, his first wife died. In 1947, he married writer [[Julia Namier|Julia de Beausobre]] (born '''Iulia Michaelovna Kazarina'''), who authored a biography of him after his death.<ref name="Colley 1989 x"/> To marry Julia, who was a convinced Christian, he converted to Anglicanism which led to an estrangement with Chaim Weizman.<ref name="Colley 1989 18">{{Harvnb|Colley|1989|p=18}}</ref>

His sister, Teodora Niemirowska, was the mother of [[Anna Kurska]].

== Political views ==
{{More citations needed|section|date=January 2023}}
Namier is best known for his work on the [[Parliament of Great Britain]], in particular English politics in the 1760s.<ref name = "Mansfield 1962 28">{{Harvnb|Mansfield|1962|p=28}}.</ref> His principal conclusion of that decade was that there was no risk of an authoritarian disposal of British parliamentarism. By way of its very detailed study of individuals, this course of study caused substantial revision to accounts based on a party system. Namier's best-known works were ''The Structure of Politics at the Accession of George III'', ''England in the Age of the American Revolution'' and the ''[[History of Parliament]]'' series he edited later in his life with [[John Brooke (British historian)|John Brooke]].

Namier used [[prosopography]] or collective biography of every Member of Parliament (MP) and peer who sat in the British Parliament in the latter 18th century to reveal that local interests, not national ones, often determined how parliamentarians voted. Namier argued very strongly that far from being tightly organised groups, both the [[Tories (British political party)|Tories]] and [[British Whig Party|Whigs]] were collections of ever-shifting and fluid small groups whose stances altered on an issue-by-issue basis. Namier felt that prosopographical methods were the best for analysing small groups like the [[British House of Commons|House of Commons]], but he was opposed to the application of prosopography to larger groups. At the time of its publication in 1929, ''The Structure of Politics at the Accession of George III'' caused a historiographical revolution in understanding the 18th century.

"What Namier's minutely detailed studies revealed was the fact that politics in 1760 consisted mainly in the jockeying for position and influence by individuals within the political elite" rather than ideas such as liberty or democracy, or rivalry with foreign kings, or social effects of industrial and technological change. "Spending many years himself, off and on, in psychoanalysis, [Namier] believed that the "deep-seated drives and emotions" of the individual were what explained politics", wrote [[Richard J. Evans]] on 29 November 2019, reviewing ''Conservative Revolutionary: the lives of Lewis Namier'' (Manchester University Press, 2019), a new biography by D. W. Hayton (a participant in the [[History of Parliament]] project).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.the-tls.co.uk/articles/geniuses-dont-have-to-be-nice/|title = Geniuses don't have to be nice|accessdate=19 January 2023}}</ref>

===Controversies===
Namier used sources such as wills and tax records to reveal the interests of the MPs. In his time, his methods were new and quite controversial. His obsession with collecting facts such as club membership of various MPs and then attempting to correlate them with voting patterns led his critics to accuse him of "taking ideas out of history".<ref>Malin Dahlstrom (2011), [http://www.history.ac.uk/reviews/review/1133 The Life and Thought of Herbert Butterfield] (book review). Namier's characterisation has been wrongly attributed to Herbert Butterfield, but was actually written by  A. J. P. Taylor. ''Reviews in History.''</ref> Namier has been described by the historian [[Lawrence Stone]] as a member of an 'elitist school' with a 'deeply pessimistic attitude toward human affairs'.<ref name="LS54">{{cite book |title=The Past & the Present Revisited |author=Lawrence Stone |publisher=Routledge, imprint of Taylor & Francis |year=2014 |orig-year=1987 |isbn=978-1136879265 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=4AYSBAAAQBAJ&q=Lewis+Namier+self-interest+elite&pg=PA54 |page=54}}</ref>

His biographer John Cannon concludes:
:Namier's achievements were greatly praised during his lifetime and unduly disparaged subsequently. On his chosen ground, the accession of George III, he made important and probably irreversible corrections to the traditional whiggish account....Later on Namier was not so much repudiated as outflanked, by critics who pointed to the narrowness of his concerns, and his lack of interest in anything but political history. The technique of structural analysis, with which his name was inextricably linked as 'Namierism', offered, in his view, an escape from voluminous narrative....[but] its limitations are very evident. There are great swathes of history where, for lack of evidence, structural analysis can hardly be applied. Even where it can, there is no guarantee that it will, in itself, generate interesting and important questions.<ref>John Cannon, 'Namier, Sir Lewis Bernstein (1888–1960)', ''Oxford Dictionary of National Biography'' (2004) [http://www.oxforddnb.com/view/article/35183, accessed 8 Oct 2017]</ref>

===Diplomatic history controversies===
As a former patient of [[Sigmund Freud]], Namier was a believer in [[psychohistory]]. He also wrote on modern European history, especially [[diplomatic history]] and his later books ''Europe in Decay'', ''In the Nazi Era'' and ''Diplomatic Prelude'' unsparingly condemned the [[Nazi Germany|Third Reich]] and [[appeasement]]. In the 1930s, Namier had been active in the anti-appeasement movement and together with his protégé [[A. J. P. Taylor]] spoke out against the [[Munich Agreement]] at several rallies in 1938. In the early 1950s, Namier had a celebrated debate on the pages of the ''[[Times Literary Supplement]]'' with the former French foreign minister [[Georges Bonnet]].<ref name="Adamthwaite, Anthony pages 183-184">{{Harvnb|Adamthwaite|1977|pp=183–184}}</ref>

At issue was the question whether Bonnet had, as Namier charged, snubbed an offer by the Polish foreign minister Colonel [[Józef Beck]] in May 1938 to have Poland come to the aid of [[Czechoslovakia]] in the event of a German attack. Bonnet denied that such an offer had been made, which led Namier to accuse Bonnet of seeking to falsify the record.<ref name="Adamthwaite, Anthony pages 183-184"/>

Namier concluded the debate in 1953 with words "The Polish offer, for what it was worth, was first torpedoed by Bonnet the statesmen, and next obliterated by Bonnet the historian".<ref>{{Harvnb|Adamthwaite|1977|p=184}}</ref>

Namier's writings on German history have been criticised for being influenced by [[Germanophobia]].<ref>{{Harvnb|Crozier|1997|p=226}}</ref> His hatred of Germany was legendary; Namier himself wrote in 1942 as the war raged on: "it did not require either 1914, or 1933, or 1939 to teach me the truth about the Germans. Long before the last war I considered them a deadly menace to Europe and the civilisation."<ref>{{Harvnb|Wrigley|2006|p=[https://books.google.com/books?id=fwNQEkGp29cC&pg=PA70 70]}}</ref>

Like the work of his friend Sir [[John Wheeler-Bennett]], Namier's [[diplomatic history|diplomatic histories]] are generally poorly regarded by modern historians because he was content to condemn appeasement without seeking to explain the reasons for it; and eager to dismiss political principles as rhetorical posturing.<ref name="JS">{{cite journal |year=2015 |publisher=History Publications |journal=History Ireland |location=Dublin |title=Two treatises of government |at=The problematic relationship between political ideas and political practice |author=Jim Smyth, Professor of History at the University of Notre Dame |url=http://www.historyireland.com/18th-19th-century-history/robert-emmets-copy-of-john-lockes-two-treatises-of-government/|accessdate=14 December 2022}}</ref>

contested.clean_text: thumb|right|200px| Sir Lewis Bernstein Namier (; 27 June 1888 – 19 August 1960) was a British historian of Polish-Jewish background. His best-known works were The Structure of Politics at the Accession of George III (1929), England in the Age of the American Revolution (1930) and the History of Parliament series (begun 1940) he edited later in his life with John Brooke. Namier was born Ludwik Bernstein Niemirowski in Wola Okrzejska in the Russian-controlled Congress Poland, now part of the Lublin Voivodeship of southeastern Poland, although his family moved to Austrian Poland in 1890. His family were secular-minded Polish-Jewish gentry. His father, with whom young Lewis often quarreled, idolized the Austro-Hungarian Empire. By contrast, Namier throughout his life detested it. Before coming to England he was educated at the University of Lwów in Austrian Galicia (now in Ukraine) and the University of Lausanne. At Lausanne, Namier heard Vilfredo Pareto lecture, and Pareto's ideas about elites would have a great influence on his thinking. Namier emigrated to the United Kingdom in 1907, studied first at the London School of Economics for a year and then at Balliol College, Oxford, from 1908, and became a British subject in 1913, whereupon he anglicised his name. Professor Piotr Eberhardt from the Polish Academy of Sciences speculates that Lloyd George could have been aware of Namier's modification; The earlier-approved compromised version of the Curzon Line which was approved at the Spa Conference in Belgium was renamed by Namier as Curzon Line "B". Chicherin relayed this document to Lenin who rejected it nevertheless, assured of his victory over Poland followed by a planned annexation of its entire territory. In one of his memoranda Namier falsified the results of a national census from Eastern Galicia originating from Austria-Hungary. He single-handedly reduced the number of ethnic Poles living in the region from 2 million down to 600–700 inhabitants. Professor Anna M. Cienciala believes that Namier was not the original initiator of this misrepresentation, but merely an unscrupulous supplier of handy arguments for the anti-Polish lobby among the Entente members. After leaving government service, Namier taught at Balliol (1920–21) before going into business for himself. Later Namier, who was a long-time Zionist, worked as political secretary for the Jewish Agency in Palestine (1929–31). For a time he was a close friend and associate of Chaim Weizmann, but Weizmann later severed relations with Namier when the latter converted to Anglicanism to marry his second wife. Namier served as professor at the University of Manchester from 1931 until his retirement in 1953, having been loudly cheered by his students at the conclusion of his last lecture there on European History. Namier remained active in various Zionist groups (in particular, lobbying the British government to allow the creation of what he called a Jewish Fighting Force in the Mandate of Palestine) and from 1933 was engaged in efforts on behalf of Jewish refugees from Germany. He was married twice and knighted in 1952 at the onset of Cold War. Also in 1952, Namier was given the honour of delivering the Romanes Lecture, on which subject Namier chose Monarchy and the Party System. In 1917, Namier married a Russian refugee, Clara Sophia Edeleff-Poniatowska, although they separated in 1921. He was by this time estranged from his father so that when his father died in 1922 the family estate was left to Namier's sister. In 1940 when he feared a Nazi invasion of Britain he burned a large number of his papers and arranged to commit suicide. In 1945, his first wife died. In 1947, he married writer Julia de Beausobre (born Iulia Michaelovna Kazarina), who authored a biography of him after his death. His sister, Teodora Niemirowska, was the mother of Anna Kurska. Namier is best known for his work on the Parliament of Great Britain, in particular English politics in the 1760s. His principal conclusion of that decade was that there was no risk of an authoritarian disposal of British parliamentarism. By way of its very detailed study of individuals, this course of study caused substantial revision to accounts based on a party system. Namier's best-known works were The Structure of Politics at the Accession of George III, England in the Age of the American Revolution and the History of Parliament series he edited later in his life with John Brooke. Namier used prosopography or collective biography of every Member of Parliament (MP) and peer who sat in the British Parliament in the latter 18th century to reveal that local interests, not national ones, often determined how parliamentarians voted. Namier argued very strongly that far from being tightly organised groups, both the Tories and Whigs were collections of ever-shifting and fluid small groups whose stances altered on an issue-by-issue basis. Namier felt that prosopographical methods were the best for analysing small groups like the House of Commons, but he was opposed to the application of prosopography to larger groups. At the time of its publication in 1929, The Structure of Politics at the Accession of George III caused a historiographical revolution in understanding the 18th century. "What Namier's minutely detailed studies revealed was the fact that politics in 1760 consisted mainly in the jockeying for position and influence by individuals within the political elite" rather than ideas such as liberty or democracy, or rivalry with foreign kings, or social effects of industrial and technological change. "Spending many years himself, off and on, in psychoanalysis, [Namier] believed that the "deep-seated drives and emotions" of the individual were what explained politics", wrote Richard J. Evans on 29 November 2019, reviewing Conservative Revolutionary: the lives of Lewis Namier (Manchester University Press, 2019), a new biography by D. W. Hayton (a participant in the History of Parliament project). Namier used sources such as wills and tax records to reveal the interests of the MPs. In his time, his methods were new and quite controversial. His obsession with collecting facts such as club membership of various MPs and then attempting to correlate them with voting patterns led his critics to accuse him of "taking ideas out of history". Namier has been described by the historian Lawrence Stone as a member of an 'elitist school' with a 'deeply pessimistic attitude toward human affairs'. His biographer John Cannon concludes: :Namier's achievements were greatly praised during his lifetime and unduly disparaged subsequently. On his chosen ground, the accession of George III, he made important and probably irreversible corrections to the traditional whiggish account....Later on Namier was not so much repudiated as outflanked, by critics who pointed to the narrowness of his concerns, and his lack of interest in anything but political history. The technique of structural analysis, with which his name was inextricably linked as 'Namierism', offered, in his view, an escape from voluminous narrative....[but] its limitations are very evident. There are great swathes of history where, for lack of evidence, structural analysis can hardly be applied. Even where it can, there is no guarantee that it will, in itself, generate interesting and important questions. As a former patient of Sigmund Freud, Namier was a believer in psychohistory. He also wrote on modern European history, especially diplomatic history and his later books Europe in Decay, In the Nazi Era and Diplomatic Prelude unsparingly condemned the Third Reich and appeasement. In the 1930s, Namier had been active in the anti-appeasement movement and together with his protégé A. J. P. Taylor spoke out against the Munich Agreement at several rallies in 1938. In the early 1950s, Namier had a celebrated debate on the pages of the Times Literary Supplement with the former French foreign minister Georges Bonnet. At issue was the question whether Bonnet had, as Namier charged, snubbed an offer by the Polish foreign minister Colonel Józef Beck in May 1938 to have Poland come to the aid of Czechoslovakia in the event of a German attack. Bonnet denied that such an offer had been made, which led Namier to accuse Bonnet of seeking to falsify the record. Namier's writings on German history have been criticised for being influenced by Germanophobia. His hatred of Germany was legendary; Namier himself wrote in 1942 as the war raged on: "it did not require either 1914, or 1933, or 1939 to teach me the truth about the Germans. Long before the last war I considered them a deadly menace to Europe and the civilisation." Like the work of his friend Sir John Wheeler-Bennett, Namier's diplomatic histories are generally poorly regarded by modern historians because he was content to condemn appeasement without seeking to explain the reasons for it; and eager to dismiss political principles as rhetorical posturing. ;Cold War era *The House of Commons, 1754–1790 (3 vols.), 1966 [1964], edited by John Brooke & Sir Lewis Namier. *Crossroads of Power: Essays on Eighteenth-Century England, 1962. *Charles Townshend, His Character and Career, Leslie Stephen Lecture, Cambridge University Press, 1959. *Vanished Supremacies: Essays on European History, 1812–1918, 1958. *Personalities and Powers, 1955. *Basic Factors in Nineteenth-Century European History, 1953. *Monarchy and the Party System: The Romanes Lecture Delivered in the Sheldonian Theatre 15 May 1952, 1952. *In the Nazi Era, 1952. *Avenues of History, 1952. *Europe in Decay: A Study in Disintegration, 1936–1940, 1950. *Diplomatic Prelude, 1938–1939, 1948. *[https://archive.org/details/facingeastessays00nami Facing East: Essays on Germany, the Balkans and Russia in the Twentieth Century], 1947. ;World War II period *1848: The Revolution of the Intellectuals, 1944. *Conflicts: Studies in Contemporary History, 1942. *[https://www.fadedpage.com/showbook.php?pid=201410D9 In the Margin of History], 1939. ;Interwar years *Skyscrapers and other Essays, 1931. {{WikiProject banner shell|class=GA|1= {{WikiProject Football|importance=low |England=yes |ManUtd=High |season=yes}} {{WikiProject United Kingdom |importance=Low}} }} {{Article history |action1=PR |action1date=07:30, 18 May 2011 |action1link=Wikipedia:Peer review/1998–99 Manchester United F.C. season/archive1 |action1result=reviewed |action1oldid=429582365 |action2=GAN |action2date=12:49, 10 October 2011 (UTC) |action2link=Talk:1998–99 Manchester United F.C. season/GA1 |action2result=listed |action2oldid=454863252 |action3=PR |action3date=20:22, 16 February 2012 |action3link=Wikipedia:Peer review/1998–99 Manchester United F.C. season/archive2 |action3result=reviewed |action3oldid=472513584 |action4=GTC |action4date=22:52, 14 August 2021 (UTC) |action4link=Wikipedia:Featured and good topic candidates/1998–99 Manchester United F.C. season/archive1 |action4result=promoted |ftname=1998–99 Manchester United F.C. season |ftmain=yes |otd1date=2019-05-26|otd1oldid=898914921 |otd2date=2024-05-26|otd2oldid=1225642343 |otd3date=2025-05-26|otd3oldid=1292249640 |action5 = GAR |action5date = 11:12, 3 June 2025 (UTC) |action5link = Wikipedia:Good article reassessment/1998–99 Manchester United F.C. season/1 |action5result = delisted |action5oldid = 1292102803 |action6 = GTR |action6date = 19:32, 24 December 2025 (UTC) |action6link = Wikipedia:Featured topic removal candidates/1998–99 Manchester United F.C. season/archive1 |action6result = kept |action7 = GAN |action7date = 22:17, 7 January 2026 (UTC) |action7link = Talk:1998–99 Manchester United F.C. season/GA2 |action7result = listed |action7oldid = 1331728536 |currentstatus = GA |topic = Sports and recreation }} ==Merge text== ''Text from [[Manchester United 1986-1999]] to be merged in if necessary:'' ===The treble=== The season started poorly with a 3-0 defeat to Arsenal in the Charity Shield, but United improved as the season went on. They were unbeaten in any competition from December 1998 until the end of the season. In April 1999, they met Arsenal in what was the last ever, and is commonly regarded as the greatest ever, [[FA Cup]] semi-final replay. With United playing with only ten men after captain [[Roy Keane]] was sent off, [[Peter Schmeichel]] brilliantly saved a 90th minute penalty kick. [[Ryan Giggs]] scored the winning goal in extra time with a breathtaking run followed by a spectacular goal. United followed this victory up with a legendary comeback victory against [[Juventus]], inspired by captain [[Roy Keane]], to book their place in the [[UEFA Champions League]] final. The League was then clinched at home to [[Tottenham Hotspur F.C.|Tottenham]] in United's final match of the competition. They then won the [[FA Cup]] with a 2-0 win over [[Newcastle United F.C.|Newcastle]], before the trip to Barcelona's [[Camp Nou|Nou Camp]] to face [[Bayern Munich]] in the final of the [[UEFA Champions League]], on the day which would have been Sir [[Matt Busby]]'s 90th birthday. Bayern Munich scored from an early [[Mario Basler]] free-kick and United fought the rest of the game to no avail, in fact Bayern should have scored again, hitting the woodwork twice. At 90 minutes, with the fourth official signalling 4 minutes of injury time, United won a corner and pulled out all the stops, even sending up goalie [[Peter Schmeichel]], who, with [[Roy Keane]] suspended, was serving as captain in his last ever appearance for the club. Bayern Munich were in shock as [[Teddy Sheringham]] squeezed in the equaliser. Less than a minute later, United won another corner and [[Ole Gunnar Solskjær]] won it all with a goal to send the United fans into delirium and cap off an incredible comeback. Manchester United became the first English team to win the Premiership/FA Cup/Champions League [[The Treble|treble]]. That summer, Alex Ferguson was awarded a knighthood for his contribution to United's success. Their achievement in the Champions League final was ranked 4th in Channel 4's [[100 Greatest Sporting Moments]] in 2002. Ryan Giggs' goal in the FA Cup Semi Final replay was ranked 23rd. [[User:Qwghlm|Qwghlm]] 11:56, 17 January 2006 (UTC) :I'd be the first to admit my view on the subject is far from neutral, but I removed part of the sentence "A crowd reported to be over three-quarters of a million people lined the streets,including fans from bitter rivals [[Manchester City]] and [[Liverpool F.C]] , to cheer them on.". No self-respecting City or Liverpool fan would do so, and I would not accept the claim without a non-partisan source. stable.clean_text: {{WikiProject banner shell|class=GA|1= }} Text from Manchester United 1986-1999 to be merged in if necessary: The season started poorly with a 3-0 defeat to Arsenal in the Charity Shield, but United improved as the season went on. They were unbeaten in any competition from December 1998 until the end of the season. In April 1999, they met Arsenal in what was the last ever, and is commonly regarded as the greatest ever, FA Cup semi-final replay. With United playing with only ten men after captain Roy Keane was sent off, Peter Schmeichel brilliantly saved a 90th minute penalty kick. Ryan Giggs scored the winning goal in extra time with a breathtaking run followed by a spectacular goal. United followed this victory up with a legendary comeback victory against Juventus, inspired by captain Roy Keane, to book their place in the UEFA Champions League final. The League was then clinched at home to Tottenham in United's final match of the competition. They then won the FA Cup with a 2-0 win over Newcastle, before the trip to Barcelona's Nou Camp to face Bayern Munich in the final of the UEFA Champions League, on the day which would have been Sir Matt Busby's 90th birthday. Bayern Munich scored from an early Mario Basler free-kick and United fought the rest of the game to no avail, in fact Bayern should have scored again, hitting the woodwork twice. At 90 minutes, with the fourth official signalling 4 minutes of injury time, United won a corner and pulled out all the stops, even sending up goalie Peter Schmeichel, who, with Roy Keane suspended, was serving as captain in his last ever appearance for the club. Bayern Munich were in shock as Teddy Sheringham squeezed in the equaliser. Less than a minute later, United won another corner and Ole Gunnar Solskjær won it all with a goal to send the United fans into delirium and cap off an incredible comeback. Manchester United became the first English team to win the Premiership/FA Cup/Champions League treble. That summer, Alex Ferguson was awarded a knighthood for his contribution to United's success. Their achievement in the Champions League final was ranked 4th in Channel 4's 100 Greatest Sporting Moments in 2002. Ryan Giggs' goal in the FA Cup Semi Final replay was ranked 23rd. 24px CTOAGN (talk) 00:40, 17 January 2006 (UTC) ---- :The treble season is unique by its own name; three the magic number. An introductory sentence like that sets the tone for this article - fanboyish, not encyclopaedic. Should be rewritten to a less gushing, more NPOV style. No self-respecting City or Liverpool fan would do so, and I would not accept the claim without a non-partisan source. I realise this means removing a lot of the content that was there already, but it is my opinion. – PeeJay 22:49, 11 June 2012 (UTC) :::"stated that entering both tournaments would overload their fixture schedule and make it more difficult to defend their Champions League and Premiership titles, and claimed that they did not want to devalue the FA Cup by fielding a weaker side" is a paraphrase of The FA's press release. [http://www.independent.co.uk/sport/football/premier-league/united-underline-cup-withdrawal-742549.html] This provides a bit more insight into the controversy. :::If the Goal.com poll had ranked the 1999 Champions League Final first, would you have included it? :::MonkeyKingBar (talk) 14:47, 12 June 2012 (UTC) ::::No, I would not have included it because I place ZERO value on anything that comes out of that website. The people who work there are hack journalists who do nothing but rehash stories already reported by others in the reliable media and create pointless polls as their only form of original content. – PeeJay 18:06, 12 June 2012 (UTC) :Can you stop making hash changes? As PeeJay has stated, Goal.com is tripe. Their poll holds zero significance. I don't care if they said first, third or 15th, the website's writers are more likely to have done their research on Wikipedia first and added bogus information to beef up their articles, which might have been unsourced. This is a good article as of now -- I'd rather keep it like this then add unsourced information ruin it with bad poor prose. -- Lemonade51 (talk) 14:57, 12 June 2012 (UTC) ::How are more details from the The FA's press release ruining the article in any way? MonkeyKingBar (talk) 15:43, 12 June 2012 (UTC) :::Per WP:OFFTOPIC -- that is more suited to 1999–2000 Manchester United F.C. season. The FA press release has not much relevance to this article; by saying "Ferguson has since admitted his regrets in how they handled the situation" is a better way of putting a closure on the subject. -- Lemonade51 (talk) 15:56, 12 June 2012 (UTC) It has been a while since this article has been reviewed, so I took a look at it and saw that there were uncited statements in the article, including entire paragraphs. Should this article go to WP:GAR? Z1720 (talk) 02:22, 24 April 2025 (UTC)

contested.title: Jack Sarfatti
contested.raw_text:
{{short description|American theoretical physicist}}
{{distinguish|Jonathan Sarfati}}
{{multiple issues|
{{Cleanup weighted|date=December 2023}}
{{POV|date=December 2023}}
}}
{{pp-move}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=December 2023}}
{{Infobox academic
| name = Jack Sarfatti
| image = Jack Sarfatti (cropped) 2.jpg
| image_size = 
| alt = 
| caption = Sarfatti in 2007
| birth_name = Jack Sarfatt
| birth_date = {{Birth date and age|1939|9|14|mf=y}}<ref name=Schwartz5>Stephen Schwartz, [http://www.sfgate.com/news/article/SUNDAY-INTERVIEW-The-Universe-As-Seen-From-3774064.php "The Universe, As Seen From North Beach"], ''San Francisco Chronicle'', August 17, 1997, p. 5.</ref>
| birth_place = [[New York City]], US
| education = {{ubl | [[Cornell University]] | [[University of California, San Diego]] | [[University of California, Riverside]] }}
| thesis_title = Gauge Invariance in the Theory of Superfluidity
| thesis_year = 1969
| doctoral_advisor = [[Fred Cummings]]
| workplaces = [[San Diego State University]]
| website = [http://stardrive.org stardrive.org]
}}

'''Jack Sarfatti''' (born September 14, 1939) is an American [[Theoretical physics|theoretical physicist]]. Working largely outside academia, most of Sarfatti's publications revolve around [[quantum physics]] and [[consciousness]].

Sarfatti was a leading member of the [[Fundamental Fysiks Group]], an informal group of physicists in California in the 1970s who, according to historian of science [[David Kaiser (physicist)|David Kaiser]], aimed to inspire some of the investigations into quantum physics that underlie parts of [[quantum information science]].<ref>Kaiser 2011, p. xxiiiff; [[David Kaiser (physicist)|David Kaiser]], [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0Lpf15w6voc "Lecture: How the Hippies Saved Physics"], WGBH PBS, April 28, 2010 (hereafter Kaiser 2010), from 04:00 mins, particularly from 11:00 mins.{{paragraph break}}
{{cite journal |doi=10.1038/476278a |author-link=Hugh Gusterson|title=Physics: Quantum outsiders|year=2011|last1=Gusterson|first1=Hugh|journal=Nature|volume=476|issue=7360|pages=278–279|bibcode=2011Natur.476..278G|s2cid=4347880|doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=Johnson2011>[[George Johnson (writer)|George Johnson]], [https://www.nytimes.com/2011/06/19/books/review/book-review-how-the-hippies-saved-physics-by-david-kaiser.html?pagewanted=all "What Physics Owes the Counterculture"], ''The New York Times'', June 17, 2011.</ref> Sarfatti co-wrote ''Space-Time and Beyond'' (1975; with Bob Toben and [[Fred Alan Wolf]]) and has published several books.<ref>For Sarfatti's authorship of ''Space-Time and Beyond'', Kaiser 2011, p. 136; Rosen 1994, p.  [https://books.google.com/books?id=mCwh4h5bKaQC&pg=PA141 141]; also see [https://web.archive.org/web/20110716205057/http://forum-network.org/lecture/how-hippies-saved-physics Kaiser 2010], from 23:22 mins.</ref>

==Early life and education==
Jack Sarfatt was born in [[Brooklyn, New York]], to Hyman and Millie Sarfatti and raised in the borough's [[Midwood]] neighborhood.<ref>[https://books.google.com/books?id=ZeLiAAAAMAAJ&q=Hyman+Sarfatti ''Technology Review''], Association of Alumni and Alumnae of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, 1976, p. 1; Jack Sarfatti, ''Destiny Matrix''. AuthorHouse, 2002, p. 93.</ref> His father was born in [[Kastoria]], Greece, and moved to New York as a child with his family.<ref>{{cite journal |first=Hyman |last=Sarfatti |url=http://scigod.com/index.php/sgj/article/download/342/387 |title=My Story: Cosmic Consciousness & Me |journal=Scientific GOD Journal |volume=5 |issue=8 |date=October 2014 |pages=660–682 [660, 664]}}</ref>

After graduating from [[Midwood High School]] in 1956, Sarfatt attended [[Cornell University]], where he received a [[Bachelor of Arts|B.A.]] in physics in 1960. Following graduate studies at Cornell and [[Brandeis University]],<ref name=Burns>Alex Burns, [http://www.21cmagazine.com/383372/Jack-Sarfatti-Weird-Science "Jack Sarfatti: Weird Science"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140226213914/http://www.21cmagazine.com/383372/Jack-Sarfatti-Weird-Science |date=February 26, 2014 }}, 21C magazine, 1996.</ref> he obtained an [[M.S.]] in 1967 from the [[University of California, San Diego]] and a [[Ph.D.]] in 1969 from the [[University of California, Riverside]] under [[Fred Cummings]], both in physics; his dissertation was "Gauge Invariance in the Theory of Superfluidity."<ref>For the MS, [http://www.sfgate.com/cgi-bin/article.cgi?f=/c/a/1997/08/17/SC46892.DTL&ao=5 Schwartz 1997]{{dead link|date=February 2026|bot=medic}}{{cbignore|bot=medic}}, p. 5; for the PhD, Jack Sarfatt[i], [http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/1969PhDT.......175S "Gauge Invariance in the Theory of Superfluidity"], The Smithsonian/NASA Astrophysics Data System.</ref>

==Career==
From 1967 to 1971, Sarfatt was an [[assistant professor]] of physics at [[San Diego State University]]. He also studied at the Cornell Space Science Center, the UK [[Atomic Energy Research Establishment]], the [[Max Planck Institute for Physics]], and [[International Centre for Theoretical Physics]].<ref name=Burns/><ref>"If the beer don't get you, then the black holes must," ''New Scientist'', October 18, 1973, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=RWRmXO0ut6YC&pg=PA165 165].</ref> Then he decided to leave academia around the time when he was in [[Trieste]].

In the 1970s, he changed the spelling of his name from Sarfatt to Sarfatti.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kaiser |first=David |url=https://www.google.fr/books/edition/How_the_Hippies_Saved_Physics_Science_Co/mkO1PGlMzS0C?hl=fr&gbpv=1&dq=jack+sarfatti+%22sarfatt%22+changed+his+name&pg=PT346&printsec=frontcover |title=How the Hippies Saved Physics: Science, Counterculture, and the Quantum Revival |date=2011-06-27 |publisher=W. W. Norton & Company |isbn=978-0-393-08230-2 |language=en}}</ref>

Sarfatti was invited to help shape the [[100 Year Starship]] program.<ref>{{cite web | last=Weinberger | first=Sharon | title=100 Year Starship: An interstellar leap for mankind? | website=BBC Future | date=March 21, 2012 | url=https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20120321-searching-for-a-starship | access-date=May 28, 2022}}</ref>

Sarfatti's ideas relating quantum physics to [[David Chalmers]]'s "[[Hard problem of consciousness]]" (i.e., how our conscious experiences are generated) are mentioned in a paper by [[Paavo Pylkkänen]].<ref name="Pylkkänen 2022">{{cite book | last=Pylkkänen | first=Paavo | date=July 2, 2022 | chapter-url=https://philpapers.org/rec/PYLITB | chapter=Is the Brain Analogous to a Quantum Measuring Apparatus? | pages=215–235 | title=Metaphors and Analogies in Sciences and Humanities | url=https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-030-90688-7 | isbn=978-3-030-90688-7 | series=Synthese Library | volume=453 |editor1=Shyam Wuppuluri | editor2=Anthony C. Grayling |publisher=[[Springer Science+Business Media]] | doi=10.1007/978-3-030-90688-7 | s2cid=249219812 | access-date=January 1, 2023}}</ref>

Sarfatti claims to have been recruited by agents of the [[CIA]] and [[United States Department of Defense|DOD]] to work on both the physics of consciousness and the propulsion of "flying saucers" back in the 1970s.{{citation needed|date=January 2023}} MIT professor David Kaiser mentions these connections in his book ''How the Hippies Saved Physics''. As evidence Sarfatti cites a recording of his 1973 meeting with [[Harold E. Puthoff]], [[Russell Targ]], and others on his visit to Stanford Research Institute.<ref>{{cite web | last=Sarfatti | first=Jack | title=1973 "CIA" recording Sarfatti, Puthoff, Targ, O Regan SRI UFO from Future | website=SoundCloud | date=March 10, 2020 | url=https://soundcloud.com/jack-sarfatti/cia-recording-sarfatti-puthoff-targ-o-regan-sri-ufo-from-future | access-date=January 1, 2023}}</ref>

Sarfatti's name appears in several released CIA documents including a summary for the STARGATE project for remote viewing published June 1, 1979.<ref>{{Cite web | title=Search {{!}} CIA FOIA (foia.cia.gov) | url=https://www.cia.gov/readingroom/search/site/sarfatti | access-date=2025-01-16 | website=www.cia.gov}}</ref>

===Politics===
According to Kaiser, Sarfatti's politics have leaned to the right since the early 1980s, when he became dependent on a cadre of "politically conservative thinkers who were drawn to certain [[New Age]] ideas" for research funding, following the dissolution of his relationship with [[Werner Erhard]].<ref>Kaiser 2011, p. 63.</ref>

==Other activities==
A longtime habitué of [[Caffe Trieste]], Sarfatti has been involved in the [[Fundamental Fysiks Group]], the [[Esalen Institute]]-affiliated Physics–Consciousness Research Group, and the [[100 Year Starship]] project.<ref>H. M. Collins and T. J. Pinch, ''Frames of Meaning: The Social Construction of Extraordinary Science'', Routledge, 2013, p. [https://books.google.com/books?id=MmmZDF8nepUC&pg=PA189 189], n. 4.</ref><ref>Kaiser 2011, pp. 15, 298, n. 18.</ref>

==Selected works==
*Toben, Bob (1975). ''Space-Time and Beyond: Toward an Explanation of the Unexplainable''. E.P. Dutton (Toben in conversation with Fred Alan Wolf and Jack Sarfatti). {{ISBN|978-0-525-47399-2}}
* {{cite journal | last2=Stoneham |first2=A.M. |last1=Sarfatti |first1=J. |date=1966 |title=The Goldstone Theorem and the Jahn-Teller Effect |url=https://discovery.ucl.ac.uk/id/eprint/97090/1/0370-1328_91_1_331.pdf |journal=[[Proceedings of the Physical Society]] |volume=91 |issue=1 |pages=214–221 |doi=10.1088/0370-1328/91/1/331}}
* {{cite journal |doi=10.1007/s41470-019-00060-1 |title=Henry Stapp's Influence on My Post-Quantum Mechanics of Consciousness Via Locally Decodable Keyless Entanglement Signaling |year=2020 |last1=Sarfatti |first1=Jack |s2cid=191144774 |journal=Activitas Nervosa Superior |volume=62 |pages=25–28 }}

==References==
{{reflist}}

==Sources==
* {{Cite book|last=Kaiser|first=David|title=How the Hippies Saved Physics: Science, Counterculture, and the Quantum Revival|publisher=W. W. Norton|year=2011|isbn=9780393076363|oclc=758540688|author-link=David Kaiser (physicist)}}

==Further reading==
* [http://stardrive.org Stardrive.org], Sarfatti's website
* [https://cornell.academia.edu/JackSarfatti Jack Sarfatti], academia.edu
{{Authority control}}
{{Wikiquote}}
