contested.title: Todd Andrews
contested.raw_text:
{{Short description|Irish republican and public servant (1901–1985)}} {{About|the Irish republican|the actor|Tod Andrews}} {{Use Hiberno-English|date=October 2021}} {{Use dmy dates|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox military person | name = Todd Andrews | image = Todd Andrews.jpg | birth_name = Christopher Stephen Andrews | birth_date = {{birth date|1901|10|6|df=y}} | birth_place = [[Summerhill, Dublin]], Ireland | death_date = {{death date and age|1985|10|11|1901|10|6|df=y}} | death_place = [[Phibsborough]], [[Dublin]], Ireland | allegiance = [[Republic of Ireland|Ireland]] | branch = [[Irish Republican Army (1919–1922)|Irish Republican Army]]
[[Irish Republican Army (1922–1969)|Anti-Treaty IRA]] | serviceyears = 1919–1923 | rank = | unit = | commands = | battles_label = Wars | battles = {{Ubl|[[Irish War of Independence]]|[[Irish Civil War]]}} | awards = | spouse = {{plainlist| * {{marriage|Mary Coyle|1928|1967|end=died}} * {{marriage|Joyce Duffy|1968}} }} | children = 4, including [[David Andrews (politician)|David]] and [[Niall Andrews|Niall]] | relations = {{Ubl|[[Ryan Tubridy]] (grandson)|[[Barry Andrews (politician)|Barry Andrews]] (grandson)|[[Chris Andrews (politician)|Chris Andrews]] (grandson)|[[David McSavage]] (grandson)}} | laterwork = [[Public servant]] |}} '''Christopher Stephen "Todd" Andrews''' (6 October 1901 – 11 October 1985) was an [[Irish republicanism|Irish republican]] and later a public servant. He participated in the [[Irish War of Independence]] and the [[Irish Civil War|Civil War]] but never stood for election or held public office. == Early life and education == Andrews was born at 42 [[Summerhill, Dublin|Summerhill]] in [[Dublin]] in 1901 to Christopher Andrews (then an Auctioneer) and Mary Andrews (née Moran), daughter of a [[Dublin Metropolitan Police]] inspector. In 1910 the family moved to [[Terenure]], where his father had a grocery shop.{{cite web |url=https://www.dib.ie/biography/andrews-christopher-stephen-todd-a0155 |title=Andrews, Christopher Stephen ("Todd") |website=Dictionary of Irish Biography |publisher=Royal Irish Academy |access-date=27 November 2025 }} He acquired the nickname "Todd" because of his perceived resemblance to English comic strip hero Alonzo Todd, who appeared in ''[[The Magnet]]''.{{cite journal |title=Founding Father Dr. C. S. "Todd" Andrews 1901–1985 |journal=Scéal na Móna |volume=13 |issue=41 |date=April 2002 |pages=18–21 |url=http://www.heartland.ie/articles/founding-father-dr-c-s-%E2%80%9Ctodd%E2%80%9D-andrews-1901-1985|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180115122122/http://www.heartland.ie/articles/founding-father-dr-c-s-%E2%80%9Ctodd%E2%80%9D-andrews-1901-1985 |archive-date=15 January 2018 }} Andrews briefly attended [[St. Enda's School]] and completed his secondary education at [[Synge Street CBS]].{{cite book |title=Ireland: A Reference Guide From The Renaissance To The Present |last=McCarthy |first=John P. |isbn=0-8160-5378-2 |year=2006 |pages=184–185 |publisher=Facts on File, Inc |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=8Olr8Iu9RAwC&pg=PA184}} He went on to study commerce at [[University College Dublin]], and although his studies were interrupted by his participation in the [[Irish War of Independence]] and the [[Irish Civil War]], he returned to the university where he obtained a [[Bachelor of Commerce|degree in Commerce]]. ==Nationalist revolutionary== Andrews was politicised by the [[1916 Rising]]. He joined the [[Irish Volunteers]] at the age of fifteen and had an active role in the [[Irish Republican Army (1919–1922)|Irish Republican Army]] during the [[Irish War of Independence]], fighting with the Rathfarnham company of the 4th Dublin Brigade.{{cite web |url=https://www.dib.ie/biography/andrews-christopher-stephen-todd-a0155 |title=Andrews, Christopher Stephen ('Todd') |last=Garvin |first=Tom |date=July 2013 |website=Dictionary OF Irish Biography |publisher= |access-date=11 June 2022 |quote=}} He was arrested and imprisoned in 1920 but released after ten days on hunger strike. He was interned at [[Curragh Camp|the Curragh]] in 1921 but tunnelled his way to freedom with two comrades.Garvin, p. 1 Andrews opposed the [[Anglo-Irish Treaty]] and, after the creation of the IRA's Four Courts Executive in April 1922, became assistant to Director of Operations [[Ernie O'Malley]]. He took the republican side during the [[Irish Civil War]] and was wounded in the fighting in O'Connell Street, Dublin. Andrews was appointed the IRA's General Headquarters and travelled the country supervising the training of volunteers.MacEoin, Uinseann (1997), ''The IRA in the Twilight Years 1923-1948'', Argenta Publications, Dublin, p. 137, ISBN 0951117246 Andrews was interned by the government of the [[Irish Free State]] until early 1924. ==Public servant== After graduation, Andrews found employment as an accountant with the then-fledgling Irish Tourist Association where he structured their accounts office, as well as editing several of their publications. In the summer of 1930 he was offered a position as an accountant with the [[Electricity Supply Board]] at a time when they were expanding the National Grid and constructing significant Hydro-Electric projects such as [[Shannon hydroelectric scheme|Ardnacrusha]]. In 1933, Andrews was appointed to the Department of Industry and Commerce, where he dealt with the industrialisation of Irish turf development. Andrews initially set up a network of co-ops that locally harvested and sold turf but quickly saw that this arrangement was insufficient to successfully modernise turf production in Ireland on a commercial scale; it also drew the ire of [[coal merchant]]s who worried about the effect of a State-led competition to their markets. However such worries were overcome by Andrews through shrewd and active man management, culminating with the establishment of the Turf Development Board in 1934. The new semi-state company helped overcome future issues in managing peat harvesting on a grand scale and schemes set up to help fuel Ireland during [[The Emergency (Ireland)|The Emergency]], and ultimately led to the formation of [[Bord na Móna|Bord Na Mona]] in 1946, a body that he ultimately became Chief Executive of. ==CIE== {{Multiple issues|section=yes| {{More citations needed section|date=April 2025}} {{POV section|date=April 2025}} }} In 1958, Andrews was offered and accepted the chairmanship of the Irish transport company [[Córas Iompair Éireann]] (CIÉ), which was in a perilous fiscal state. Following on from the findings of the Beddy Report,{{Cite web|last=Irish Railway Record Society|date=2020|title=CIE Fact Sheet|url=http://irishrailarchives.ie/wp-content/uploads/2018/06/IRRS_Factsheets_CIE.pdf|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=|website=www.irishrailwayarchives.ie}} he drew from his business experience and oversaw a large restructuring of the Irish rail system. This included the purchase of diesel electric engines from General Motors, the introduction of modern coaching stock, the phased closure of uneconomic services and elimination of slow stopping services, the introduction of new braked good wagons as well as a revamp of ticketing arrangements. He also oversaw the closures of several lines that were perennially uneconomic and loss-making. This included: *The Bray to [[Harcourt Street railway line]]. The closure of the Harcourt Street line is undoubtedly the most infamous railway closure in Ireland. The line had not been profitable for decades, in spite of it seeing many cost-cutting measures to try and improve business. Electric signalling, AEC railcars and summer special trains to Wicklow and Wexford had been introduced on the line to try and increase its footfall and to lower costs to no avail. On foot of a recommendation of the Beddy Report, the line ceased services on 31 December 1958 and formally abandoned in January 1959. As the city expanded outwards in the mid and late 1970s the route was kept clear and partially reopened as part of the [[Green Line (Luas)|LUAS Green Line]] in 2004. *The substantial railway network west of Cork city. This included lines to Bandon, Bantry and Macroom, and branch lines to Clonakility, Skibbereen and Kinsale. Again, the lines struggled for business and saw diminishing business as cars, buses and lorries became more affordable and able to address passenger needs more practically. *The [[Hill of Howth Tramway]], which was inherited from the [[Great Northern Railway (Ireland)|Great Northern Railway]]. This anomalous line was built around Howth Head to exploit an expected tourism boom in the seaside village that never came to be. While the line served its sparse locality quite well it never turned a profit and escaped closure several times while under the auspices of the Great Northern Railway Board. However, its infrastructure and rolling stock had not been replaced during its lifetime and, with this weighed against a need for essential cost-cutting, its closure was inevitable giving CIE's poor fiscal state and statutory requirements to become profitable. *The [[West Clare Railway]]. Again, this line's came against a backdrop of cost-cutting but unlike other narrow gauge lines it saw the introduction of a fleet of modern diesel locomotives and railcars. While the new stock improved service levels and economics of the lines considerably they weren't enough to save the line. *The [[Cahersiveen]], [[Kenmare]] and [[Kanturk]] lines. As was common with many rural railways the traffic levels on these three lines were sparse. The branches to Kanturk and Kenmare has diesel engines allocated to them too, but to no avail. Andrews also oversaw the resurgence and modernisation of CIE road transport, provincial and city bus services in Ireland. Steam traction was eliminated under his chairmanship, a cost benefit that undoubtedly saved CIE from certain collapse, while modern vans and lorries took on the delivery of freights in place of horse and carriage. Despite such economies, CIE still struggled under a state expectation that it run without subvention; an impossible task given the sparse traffic and passenger numbers in a land cropped by emigration. Issues of Partition often affected the company's operation; CIE was forced to introduce additional bus services in border areas upon the withdrawal of the Ulster Transport Authority from cross-border services, notably with the GNRB in 1958 and the County Donegal Railway Joint Committee in 1959. ==RTÉ== He retired from CIE upon his 65th birthday but before he stood down he became chairman of the [[RTÉ Authority]] at the request of [[Seán Lemass]].The Pear is Ripe, A Memoir, John Montague During his time as chairman he oversaw significant changes as the broadcaster expanded RTÉ Radio and Television, as well as the introduction of FM radio and colour television, the opening of a Belfast news desk, the beginning of the move from the GPO to a new complex at Montrose, Donnybrook. Andrews frequently rebutted Government interference in the organisation, even rebutting advances from then Minister Erskine Childers to suppress some employees who were suspected of being subversives.{{Cite web |last=Corcoran |first=Farrel |title=Government, Public Broadcasting and the Urge to Censor |url=https://doras.dcu.ie/24077/1/Corcoran%2C%20Farrel%20.pdf |access-date=2023-04-13 |website=doras.dcu.ie}} Todd resigned from RTÉ in 1970 after his son [[David Andrews (politician)|David Andrews]] was appointed Chief Whip to the [[Taoiseach]].{{Cite web|last=|first=|date=|title=David Andrews|url=https://www.culturaldiplomacy.org/academy/index.php?David-Andrews|archive-url=|archive-date=|access-date=|website=}} ==Political views== Todd Andrews was a firm advocate of state-sponsored enterprise and economic intervention, drawing inspiration from the statist approaches he observed in Germany and the Soviet Union, particularly regarding the use of peat bogs for fuel. Identifying as a radical and aligning with the leftist tendencies of early Fianna Fáil, Andrews viewed reunification of Ireland as essential to resolving the country's challenges. In his 1970s column, ''Gallimaufry'', for de Valera's ''Irish Press'', Andrews consistently argued for British withdrawal from Northern Ireland and the end of their governance claims over any part of the island. Progressive for his time, he held liberal views on issues like divorce and contraception and harboured a deep-seated antipathy toward the Catholic Church's political influence, rooted in his republicanism and his excommunication during the Civil War. Known for his uncompromising opinions, his bluntness often led to workplace tensions, with Bord na Móna employees joking about the necessity of appointing someone to mitigate the strikes his outspokenness provoked. Andrews' son Niall would inherit much of his political views.{{Cite web |url=https://www.dib.ie/biography/andrews-niall-dermott-a9468 |title=Andrews, Niall Dermott |last=Maume |first=Patrick |date=June 2012 |website= |publisher= |access-date=27 November 2024 |quote=}} ==Later life and family== He was the recipient of several [[honorary doctorate]]s and degrees from various universities. He published his autobiography in two volumes in 1979 and 1982, under the titles of ''Dublin Made Me'' and ''Man of No Property''. Andrews died in [[Dublin]] at the age of 84. Two of his sons, [[Niall Andrews]] and David Andrews, became [[Teachta Dála|TD]]s; David Andrews became [[Minister for Foreign Affairs and Trade|Minister for Foreign Affairs]]. His brother, [[Paddy Andrews]] was a football player, most notably with [[Bohemian FC|Bohemians]] who was also capped by the [[Irish Free State national football team|Irish Free State]]. Todd Andrews' grandson [[Ryan Tubridy]] was a radio presenter and television chatshow host on RTÉ, while grandsons [[Barry Andrews (politician)|Barry Andrews]] and [[Chris Andrews (politician)|Chris Andrews]] were also [[Teachta Dála|TD]]s. Another grandson is comedian [[David McSavage]]. ==Bibliography== ===Autobiography=== *''[https://archive.org/details/dublinmademeauto0000andr Dublin Made Me]'' (Lilliput, 2001) {{ISBN|978-1-901866-65-0}} *''[https://archive.org/details/manofnoproperty0000andr Man of no Property]'' (Lilliput, 2001) {{ISBN|978-1-901866-66-7}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Andrews, Todd}} [[Category:1901 births]] [[Category:1985 deaths]] [[Category:Andrews family|Todd]] [[Category:Burials at Deans Grange Cemetery]] [[Category:Irish Republican Army (1919–1922) members]] [[Category:Irish Republican Army (1922–1969) members]] [[Category:People of the Irish Civil War (Anti-Treaty side)]] [[Category:Irish republicans interned without trial]] [[Category:People from Northside, Dublin]] [[Category:RTÉ executives]] [[Category:People educated at Synge Street CBS]] [[Category:People educated at St. Enda's School]] [[Category:Alumni of University College Dublin]]
contested.clean_text: {{Infobox military person | name = Todd Andrews | image = Todd Andrews.jpg | birth_name = Christopher Stephen Andrews | birth_date = | birth_place = Summerhill, Dublin, Ireland | death_date = | death_place = Phibsborough, Dublin, Ireland | allegiance = Ireland | branch = Irish Republican Army Anti-Treaty IRA | serviceyears = 1919–1923 | rank = | unit = | commands = | battles_label = Wars | battles = | awards = | spouse = {{plainlist| * * }} | children = 4, including David and Niall | relations = | laterwork = Public servant |}} Christopher Stephen "Todd" Andrews (6 October 1901 – 11 October 1985) was an Irish republican and later a public servant. He participated in the Irish War of Independence and the Civil War but never stood for election or held public office. Andrews was born at 42 Summerhill in Dublin in 1901 to Christopher Andrews (then an Auctioneer) and Mary Andrews (née Moran), daughter of a Dublin Metropolitan Police inspector. In 1910 the family moved to Terenure, where his father had a grocery shop. He acquired the nickname "Todd" because of his perceived resemblance to English comic strip hero Alonzo Todd, who appeared in The Magnet. Andrews briefly attended St. Enda's School and completed his secondary education at Synge Street CBS. He went on to study commerce at University College Dublin, and although his studies were interrupted by his participation in the Irish War of Independence and the Irish Civil War, he returned to the university where he obtained a degree in Commerce. He was arrested and imprisoned in 1920 but released after ten days on hunger strike. He was interned at the Curragh in 1921 but tunnelled his way to freedom with two comrades. Andrews opposed the Anglo-Irish Treaty and, after the creation of the IRA's Four Courts Executive in April 1922, became assistant to Director of Operations Ernie O'Malley. He took the republican side during the Irish Civil War and was wounded in the fighting in O'Connell Street, Dublin. Andrews was appointed the IRA's General Headquarters and travelled the country supervising the training of volunteers. Andrews was interned by the government of the Irish Free State until early 1924. After graduation, Andrews found employment as an accountant with the then-fledgling Irish Tourist Association where he structured their accounts office, as well as editing several of their publications. In the summer of 1930 he was offered a position as an accountant with the Electricity Supply Board at a time when they were expanding the National Grid and constructing significant Hydro-Electric projects such as Ardnacrusha. he drew from his business experience and oversaw a large restructuring of the Irish rail system. This included the purchase of diesel electric engines from General Motors, the introduction of modern coaching stock, the phased closure of uneconomic services and elimination of slow stopping services, the introduction of new braked good wagons as well as a revamp of ticketing arrangements. He also oversaw the closures of several lines that were perennially uneconomic and loss-making. This included: *The Bray to Harcourt Street railway line. The closure of the Harcourt Street line is undoubtedly the most infamous railway closure in Ireland. The line had not been profitable for decades, in spite of it seeing many cost-cutting measures to try and improve business. Electric signalling, AEC railcars and summer special trains to Wicklow and Wexford had been introduced on the line to try and increase its footfall and to lower costs to no avail. On foot of a recommendation of the Beddy Report, the line ceased services on 31 December 1958 and formally abandoned in January 1959. As the city expanded outwards in the mid and late 1970s the route was kept clear and partially reopened as part of the LUAS Green Line in 2004. *The substantial railway network west of Cork city. This included lines to Bandon, Bantry and Macroom, and branch lines to Clonakility, Skibbereen and Kinsale. Again, the lines struggled for business and saw diminishing business as cars, buses and lorries became more affordable and able to address passenger needs more practically. *The Hill of Howth Tramway, which was inherited from the Great Northern Railway. This anomalous line was built around Howth Head to exploit an expected tourism boom in the seaside village that never came to be. While the line served its sparse locality quite well it never turned a profit and escaped closure several times while under the auspices of the Great Northern Railway Board. However, its infrastructure and rolling stock had not been replaced during its lifetime and, with this weighed against a need for essential cost-cutting, its closure was inevitable giving CIE's poor fiscal state and statutory requirements to become profitable. *The West Clare Railway. Again, this line's came against a backdrop of cost-cutting but unlike other narrow gauge lines it saw the introduction of a fleet of modern diesel locomotives and railcars. While the new stock improved service levels and economics of the lines considerably they weren't enough to save the line. *The Cahersiveen, Kenmare and Kanturk lines. As was common with many rural railways the traffic levels on these three lines were sparse. The branches to Kanturk and Kenmare has diesel engines allocated to them too, but to no avail. Andrews also oversaw the resurgence and modernisation of CIE road transport, provincial and city bus services in Ireland. Steam traction was eliminated under his chairmanship, a cost benefit that undoubtedly saved CIE from certain collapse, while modern vans and lorries took on the delivery of freights in place of horse and carriage. Despite such economies, CIE still struggled under a state expectation that it run without subvention; an impossible task given the sparse traffic and passenger numbers in a land cropped by emigration. Issues of Partition often affected the company's operation; CIE was forced to introduce additional bus services in border areas upon the withdrawal of the Ulster Transport Authority from cross-border services, notably with the GNRB in 1958 and the County Donegal Railway Joint Committee in 1959. During his time as chairman he oversaw significant changes as the broadcaster expanded RTÉ Radio and Television, as well as the introduction of FM radio and colour television, the opening of a Belfast news desk, the beginning of the move from the GPO to a new complex at Montrose, Donnybrook. Andrews frequently rebutted Government interference in the organisation, even rebutting advances from then Minister Erskine Childers to suppress some employees who were suspected of being subversives. Todd resigned from RTÉ in 1970 after his son David Andrews was appointed Chief Whip to the Taoiseach. Todd Andrews was a firm advocate of state-sponsored enterprise and economic intervention, drawing inspiration from the statist approaches he observed in Germany and the Soviet Union, particularly regarding the use of peat bogs for fuel. Identifying as a radical and aligning with the leftist tendencies of early Fianna Fáil, Andrews viewed reunification of Ireland as essential to resolving the country's challenges. In his 1970s column, Gallimaufry, for de Valera's Irish Press, Andrews consistently argued for British withdrawal from Northern Ireland and the end of their governance claims over any part of the island. He was the recipient of several honorary doctorates and degrees from various universities. He published his autobiography in two volumes in 1979 and 1982, under the titles of Dublin Made Me and Man of No Property. Andrews died in Dublin at the age of 84. Two of his sons, Niall Andrews and David Andrews, became TDs; David Andrews became Minister for Foreign Affairs. His brother, Paddy Andrews was a football player, most notably with Bohemians who was also capped by the Irish Free State. Todd Andrews' grandson Ryan Tubridy was a radio presenter and television chatshow host on RTÉ, while grandsons Barry Andrews and Chris Andrews were also TDs. Another grandson is comedian David McSavage. *[https://archive.org/details/dublinmademeauto0000andr Dublin Made Me] (Lilliput, 2001) *[https://archive.org/details/manofnoproperty0000andr Man of no Property] (Lilliput, 2001) Category:1901 births Category:1985 deaths Todd Category:Burials at Deans Grange Cemetery Category:Irish Republican Army (1919–1922) members Category:Irish Republican Army (1922–1969) members Category:People of the Irish Civil War (Anti-Treaty side) Category:Irish republicans interned without trial Category:People from Northside, Dublin Category:RTÉ executives Category:People educated at Synge Street CBS Category:People educated at St. Enda's School Category:Alumni of University College Dublin

stable.title: Talk:7 (New York City Subway service)
stable.raw_text: {{Article history |action1=GAN |action1date=9 May 2020 |action1link=/GA1 |action1result=listed |action1oldid=955756686 |currentstatus=GA |topic=transport |dykdate=9 June 2020|dykentry= ... that the New York City Subway's '''[[7 (New York City Subway service)|7 train]]''' has been nicknamed the "International Express" due to the ethnic diversity of communities on its route?|dyknom=Template:Did you know nominations/7 (New York City Subway service)}} {{WikiProject banner shell|class=GA| {{WikiProject Trains|importance=low|NYCS=yes|NYPT-importance=high|subway=yes|portaldykdate=November 5, 2016}} {{WikiProject New York City|importance=high}} }} ==Parallel article== Hey, what the f*** happened to all of the info here on wikipedia, about the 7 train? All I see is the conductor section, but that's about it. What gives? :You mean the stuff on [[IRT Flushing Line]]? --[[User:SPUI|SPUI]] ([[User talk:SPUI|talk]]) 4 July 2005 12:48 (UTC) ::anyone vote to merge the two articles? [[User:Dogru144|Dogru144]] 16:10, 23 August 2006 (UTC) :::I agree that the contents of [[IRT Flushing Line]] and {{NYCS service|7}} need to be rationalized. However, while the two separately exist, it is wrong to put the [[IRT Flushing Line]] into [[:Category:New York City Subway services]], and it is wrong to put {{NYCS service|7}} into [[:Category:New York City Subway lines]]. Services and lines are different things. :::There is one example in the Subway project where such a rationalization has been done. The article [[S - 42nd Street Shuttle (New York City Subway service)]] describes both the ''service'' and the ''line'' on which it operates. :::It might make sense to do so here, and also for: :::*[[BMT Canarsie Line]] and {{NYCS service|L}} :::*[[BMT Franklin Avenue Line]] and [[S - Franklin Avenue Shuttle (New York City Subway service)]]. :::Given the broader nature of the issue, you might want to propose a discussion on [[Wikipedia talk:WikiProject New York City Subway]]. In the meantime, it is wrong to exacerbate the issue by putting various duplications into the two articles. [[User:Marc Shepherd|Marc Shepherd]] 17:18, 23 August 2006 (UTC) == Cleanup Template == What cleanup is needed here? I don't see anything wrong with the spelling and grammer of the article. [[User:The Legendary Ranger|The Legendary Ranger]] 20:08, 15 June 2006 (UTC) == Is the 7 switching to R142S cars? == I read on the R142S article that the 7 will convert to CBTC operation eventually. Does this mean that the 7 line will be using those modern cars soon (or at all)? If so, when? [[User:69.117.20.134|69.117.20.134]] 03:42, 22 January 2007 (UTC) Yes, I think you are right, but it's not confirmed. At this point, it shouldn't be mentioned. [[User:Asiansaxboy101|Asiansaxboy101]] 17:59, 27 March 2007 (UTC) :The 7 will be receiving [[R188 (New York City Subway car)|R188]]s in a few years (2015-16 at the earliest). The R188s will be a mix of newly-built cars and R142A conversions, and will be CBTC compatible. It should be mentioned, as the R188s have a page. [[User:Coasterlover1994|Coasterlover1994]] ([[User talk:Coasterlover1994|talk]]) 01:38, 2 July 2010 (UTC) == Picture Change == I think there should be another head picture, instead of the one right now. The "redbirds," the type train in the picture, are no longer used, and have been replaced completely with the R62's. [[User:Asiansaxboy101|Asiansaxboy101]] 18:05, 27 March 2007 (UTC) :There's a picture of R62A cars on the page. There's no need for an edit. [[User:R36|R36]] ([[User talk:R36|talk]]) 01:07, 26 July 2008 (UTC) == rolling stock question... == The article on the [[Steinway Tunnel]] says the 7 line was originally built as an "[[interurban]]" line, and consequently has (had?) a low tunnel and low weight capacity rails. Is this still true? The original tunnels of Boston's Blue Line were originally built for [[streetcar]]s, and now run (short?) subway cars. So, were the rails upgraded? Was the tunnel expanded? Does the transit commission run less than full size rolling stock on this line? [[User:Geo Swan|Geo Swan]] ([[User talk:Geo Swan|talk]]) 01:30, 15 April 2009 (UTC) :It appears that only [[Interborough Rapid Transit|IRT]] services ran through the tunnel, thus there was no incentive to upgrade the tunnel to, say, [[Brooklyn-Manhattan Transit Corporation|BMT]] standards. Virtually all or most of the IRT lines had, and will always have (at least for the foreseeable future) the narrower gauge. [[User:Tinlinkin|Tinlinkin]] ([[User talk:Tinlinkin|talk]]) 04:31, 16 May 2009 (UTC) == new 7 map/r188 order == why had not anybody update a new 7 train map or aleast it shows the extendion to 34 street the map should include details about the shell at 10 avenue. The r188 order are coming at the end of 2013 to replace the r62a from the 7 line. —Preceding [[Wikipedia:Signatures|unsigned]] comment added by [[User:Mr etre|Mr etre]] ([[User talk:Mr etre|talk]] • [[Special:Contributions/Mr etre|contribs]]) 01:04, 13 July 2010 (UTC) :It's really too soon to do that. :It is pretty hard to find [[WP:Reliable Sources|Reliable Sources]] for the 10th Avenue station, because it seems like the Bloomberg administration changes its mind every week about that station. That's why it can't be included yet. The map shows the current service and the stations listing table shows the two additional stations. [[User:Acps110|Acps110]] ([[User talk:Acps110|talk]] • [[Special:Contributions/Acps110|contribs]]) 02:23, 13 July 2010 (UTC) ::It needs to exist before it can be included. Since you can edit wiki anytime, there is no point of preparing for it when it doesn't exist it. When it does, it will be edited on the weekend it happens.--[[User:IGeMiNix|IGeMiNix]] ([[User talk:IGeMiNix|talk]]) 18:14, 15 July 2010 (UTC) == question == The NYC subway has express trains. This route has express trains. However it doesn't explain how express trains work differently than regular trains, or how both regular and express trains can share a single pair of tracks. Maybe there is some other article that explains how all the NYC express trains work. If so fine. This article should link there. If not this article needs to explain this further. [[User:Geo Swan|Geo Swan]] ([[User talk:Geo Swan|talk]]) 03:40, 10 October 2015 (UTC) :You've answered your own question. Look at the pictures. There are more than two tracks. [[User:Secondarywaltz|Secondarywaltz]] ([[User talk:Secondarywaltz|talk]]) 17:49, 11 October 2015 (UTC) == Super Express in the station listing? == Question: Even though its never on the schedule, due to it being special a post-game service, should the indication of its Super Express (making only 6 stops, terminating at Times Sq.) be mentioned in the station listings table? I want to have this discussion first before we decide on whether or not we should shade the special stops in the table, like with the A to Rockaway Park. I am fine on whichever consensus that comes from this question. [[User:Roadrunner3000|Roadrunner3000]] ([[User talk:Roadrunner3000|talk]]) 02:43, 1 April 2017 (UTC) :I'm a bit indifferent on the matter. It normally doesn't happen, and because it's not scheduled and only occurs during special events, I personally don't see the need for it to be included. But I can be persuaded otherwise if anyone feels that they should. I only support changes in service to the articles if it affects service in anyway and if it's incorporated into the system pick. I see those special trains as...special trains. Nothing else but that. —[[User:Imdanumber1|Imdanumber1]] ([[User talk:Imdanumber1|talk]] '''·''' [[Special:Contribs/Imdanumber1|contribs]] '''·''' [[Special:EmailUser/Imdanumber1|email]]) 02:51, 1 April 2017 (UTC) :I do not think it should being that those trains do not follow a specific schedule or run every day. They run based on what time Mets game ends, so they are different from the rush hour services on other trains that follow schedules. Based on YouTube videos, the MTA also runs special trains between Yankee Stadium and 2nd Avenue on the Lower East Rider before and after Yankee games, but are we going to include that too? — Preceding [[Wikipedia:Signatures|unsigned]] comment added by [[Special:Contributions/173.2.255.159|173.2.255.159]] ([[User talk:173.2.255.159#top|talk]]) 21:07, 1 April 2017 (UTC) ::You bring up a very valid point. Not to mention with those Special D trains there's no telling where they'll go. Sometimes they go to Second Avenue, but a lot of times, more often than one, they'll operate via ANY routing in South Brooklyn that's connected to Sixth Avenue to get to Coney Island. —[[User:Imdanumber1|Imdanumber1]] ([[User talk:Imdanumber1|talk]] '''·''' [[Special:Contribs/Imdanumber1|contribs]] '''·''' [[Special:EmailUser/Imdanumber1|email]]) 21:38, 1 April 2017 (UTC) :::I think we ''can'' include these Special 7 trains in the station listing, but we can't do the same with the Special D trains because of the latter's more randomized station listing. We just shade the special 7 train stops in the express service column. However, we don't need to since the prose explains the situation well enough. [[User:Epicgenius|epicgenius]] ([[User talk:Epicgenius|talk]]) 19:27, 3 April 2017 (UTC) ::::I honestly don't see the need for it, but if you do put them in, and note the super express stops by shading the boxes in, I won't object. —[[User:Imdanumber1|Imdanumber1]] ([[User talk:Imdanumber1|talk]] '''·''' [[Special:Contribs/Imdanumber1|contribs]] '''·''' [[Special:EmailUser/Imdanumber1|email]]) 00:06, 4 April 2017 (UTC) == External links modified == Hello fellow Wikipedians, I have just modified 3 external links on [[7 (New York City Subway service)]]. Please take a moment to review [https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?diff=prev&oldid=787084547 my edit]. If you have any questions, or need the bot to ignore the links, or the page altogether, please visit [[User:Cyberpower678/FaQs#InternetArchiveBot|this simple FaQ]] for additional information. I made the following changes: *Added archive https://web.archive.org/web/20120302010603/http://mta.info/mta/pdf/2010-14questions_and_answers_v2.pdf to http://mta.info/mta/pdf/2010-14questions_and_answers_v2.pdf *Added archive https://web.archive.org/web/20120711160459/http://www.ny1.com/content/news_beats/transit/162481/mta-s-7-line-extension-project-pushed-back-six-months to http://www.ny1.com/content/news_beats/transit/162481/mta-s-7-line-extension-project-pushed-back-six-months *Added archive https://web.archive.org/web/20090422043043/http://www.mta.info/nyct/service/events/mets_willets.htm to http://www.mta.info/nyct/service/events/mets_willets.htm When you have finished reviewing my changes, you may follow the instructions on the template below to fix any issues with the URLs. {{sourcecheck|checked=false|needhelp=}} Cheers.—[[User:InternetArchiveBot|'''InternetArchiveBot''']] ([[User talk:InternetArchiveBot|Report bug]]) 09:44, 23 June 2017 (UTC) == Pronunciation of <7> == How would the following sentence (from the current article) be read aloud... :The 7 and <7> run on the IRT Flushing Line in their entirety. ...? [[User:Jnestorius|jnestorius]]([[User talk:Jnestorius|talk]]) 21:20, 24 July 2025 (UTC) :{{re|jnestorius}} "<7>" can be read as either "''7 Express''" or "''Diamond 7''". [[User:Quebecguy|'''quebecguy''']] ⚜️ ([[User talk:Quebecguy|talk]] | [[Special:Contribs/Quebecguy|contribs]]) 21:25, 24 July 2025 (UTC) ::That needs to be in the article, with citations. I don't know if the existing footnote intends to make that point (about "7 express"); if so, it is unclear and not supported by the cited source. [[User:Jnestorius|jnestorius]]([[User talk:Jnestorius|talk]]) 21:44, 24 July 2025 (UTC)

Geo Swan (talk) 03:40, 10 October 2015 (UTC) :You've answered your own question. Look at the pictures. There are more than two tracks. Secondarywaltz (talk) 17:49, 11 October 2015 (UTC) Question: Even though its never on the schedule, due to it being special a post-game service, should the indication of its Super Express (making only 6 stops, terminating at Times Sq.) be mentioned in the station listings table? I want to have this discussion first before we decide on whether or not we should shade the special stops in the table, like with the A to Rockaway Park. I am fine on whichever consensus that comes from this question. Roadrunner3000 (talk) 02:43, 1 April 2017 (UTC) :I'm a bit indifferent on the matter. It normally doesn't happen, and because it's not scheduled and only occurs during special events, I personally don't see the need for it to be included. But I can be persuaded otherwise if anyone feels that they should. I only support changes in service to the articles if it affects service in anyway and if it's incorporated into the system pick. I see those special trains as...special trains. Nothing else but that. —Imdanumber1 (talk · contribs · email) 02:51, 1 April 2017 (UTC) :I do not think it should being that those trains do not follow a specific schedule or run every day. They run based on what time Mets game ends, so they are different from the rush hour services on other trains that follow schedules. Based on YouTube videos, the MTA also runs special trains between Yankee Stadium and 2nd Avenue on the Lower East Rider before and after Yankee games, but are we going to include that too? — Preceding unsigned comment added by 173.2.255.159 (talk) 21:07, 1 April 2017 (UTC) ::You bring up a very valid point. Not to mention with those Special D trains there's no telling where they'll go. Sometimes they go to Second Avenue, but a lot of times, more often than one, they'll operate via ANY routing in South Brooklyn that's connected to Sixth Avenue to get to Coney Island. —Imdanumber1 (talk · contribs · email) 21:38, 1 April 2017 (UTC) :::I think we can include these Special 7 trains in the station listing, but we can't do the same with the Special D trains because of the latter's more randomized station listing. We just shade the special 7 train stops in the express service column. However, we don't need to since the prose explains the situation well enough. epicgenius (talk) 19:27, 3 April 2017 (UTC) ::::I honestly don't see the need for it, but if you do put them in, and note the super express stops by shading the boxes in, I won't object. —Imdanumber1 (talk · contribs · email) 00:06, 4 April 2017 (UTC) Hello fellow Wikipedians, I have just modified 3 external links on 7 (New York City Subway service). Please take a moment to review [https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?diff=prev&oldid=787084547 my edit]. If you have any questions, or need the bot to ignore the links, or the page altogether, please visit this simple FaQ for additional information. I made the following changes: *Added archive https://web.archive.org/web/20120302010603/http://mta.info/mta/pdf/2010-14questions_and_answers_v2.pdf to http://mta.info/mta/pdf/2010-14questions_and_answers_v2.pdf *Added archive https://web.archive.org/web/20120711160459/http://www.ny1.com/content/news_beats/transit/162481/mta-s-7-line-extension-project-pushed-back-six-months to http://www.ny1.com/content/news_beats/transit/162481/mta-s-7-line-extension-project-pushed-back-six-months *Added archive https://web.archive.org/web/20090422043043/http://www.mta.info/nyct/service/events/mets_willets.htm to http://www.mta.info/nyct/service/events/mets_willets.htm When you have finished reviewing my changes, you may follow the instructions on the template below to fix any issues with the URLs. Cheers.—InternetArchiveBot (Report bug) 09:44, 23 June 2017 (UTC) How would the following sentence (from the current article) be read aloud... :The 7 and run on the IRT Flushing Line in their entirety. ...? jnestorius(talk) 21:20, 24 July 2025 (UTC) : "" can be read as either "7 Express" or "Diamond 7". quebecguy ⚜️ (talk | contribs) 21:25, 24 July 2025 (UTC) ::That needs to be in the article, with citations. I don't know if the existing footnote intends to make that point (about "7 express"); if so, it is unclear and not supported by the cited source. jnestorius(talk) 21:44, 24 July 2025 (UTC)

contested.title: Siliguri Corridor
contested.raw_text:
{{Short description|Corridor connecting Northeast India to the rest of India}}
{{Other uses of|Chicken's neck}}
{{POV|date=December 2025}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=July 2017}}
{{Use Indian English|date=July 2017}}
[[File:chickensneckindia.jpg|thumb|right|250px|The Siliguri Corridor is the strip of Indian territory within the red highlighted circle.]]
[[File:Siliguri Corridor sketch with Asian Highway segment.jpg|thumb|right|245x245px|[[AH2]] of the [[Asian Highway Network|Asian Highway]] goes through the Siliguri Corridor.]]
[[File:Siliguri corridor variations in measurement.jpg|thumb|242x242px|Dimensions of the corridor. Distances in kilometers.|right]]

The '''Siliguri Corridor''', often dubbed the "'''Chicken's Neck'''", is a stretch of land around the city of [[Siliguri]] in [[West Bengal]] state of [[India]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|last=Singh|first=Mayank|date=7 November 2021|title=Army steps up efforts to safeguard Siliguri Corridor|url=https://www.newindianexpress.com/thesundaystandard/2021/nov/07/army-steps-up-efforts-to-safeguard-siliguri-corridor-2380383.html|access-date=2022-01-16|website=The New Indian Express}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite web|last=Singh|first=Mohinder Pal|date=9 October 2019|title=What if China wrings India's 'Chicken's Neck' – the Siliguri corridor? Here are some countermeasures|url=https://usiofindia.org/publication/news/what-if-china-wrings-indias-chickens-neck-the-siliguri-corridor-here-are-some-countermeasures/|access-date=2022-01-16|work=The Times of India|via=USI India|archive-date=18 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220118182536/https://usiofindia.org/publication/news/what-if-china-wrings-indias-chickens-neck-the-siliguri-corridor-here-are-some-countermeasures/|url-status=dead}}</ref> It is the sole terrestrial connection between [[Mainland India|India's mainland]] and its [[Northeast India|eight northeastern states]], serving as a logistical lifeline for over 40 million people and crucial military movements.<ref name="EurasiaReview">{{cite web|title=India's New Military Bases Along the Siliguri Corridor: Strategic Defense or Signal Of Changing Regional Dynamics? – Analysis|website=Eurasia Review|url=https://www.eurasiareview.com/25112025-indias-new-military-bases-along-the-siliguri-corridor-strategic-defense-or-signal-of-changing-regional-dynamics-analysis/|date=25 November 2025}}</ref><ref name=":0"/> Bordered by [[Nepal]], [[Kingdom of Bhutan|Bhutan]] and [[Bangladesh]], it lies close to Tibet's [[Chumbi Valley]] to its northeast.<ref name=":8">{{Cite news |date=3 July 2017 |title=Chickens neck |url=https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/politics/chickens-neck/articleshow/59424396.cms |access-date=4 April 2025 |work=The Times of India |issn=0971-8257}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Sethi |first=Sunil |date=18 February 2015 |title=Status of Sikkim and its deposed monarch Palden Thondup Namgyal being questioned again |url=https://www.indiatoday.in/magazine/cover-story/story/19780430-status-of-sikkim-and-its-deposed-monarch-palden-thondup-namgyal-being-questioned-again-818652-2015-02-18 |access-date=4 April 2025 |website=India Today}}</ref><ref name="GKToday">{{cite web|title=Siliguri Corridor|website=GKToday|url=https://www.gktoday.in/siliguri-corridor/|date=31 May 2025}}</ref> The city of Siliguri is the major city in the area and the central transfer point connecting Bhutan, Nepal, Bangladesh, Sikkim, [[Darjeeling district|Darjeeling]], Northeast India and mainland India.<ref name=":8" />

==History==

The [[partition of India]] led to the formation of the Siliguri Corridor through the creation of [[East Pakistan]] (now [[Bangladesh]]) after the [[Partition of Bengal (1947)|partition of Bengal]] (into [[East Bengal]] and West Bengal) in 1947–1948.{{sfnp|Atig Ghosh, The Importance of Being Siliguri|2018|p=136}}

The [[Kingdom of Sikkim]] formerly lay on the northern side of the corridor, until its union with India in 1975 via a publicly held referendum.<ref>{{Cite news |title=Sikkim Votes On Indian Merger |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1873&dat=19750415&id=B0YfAAAAIBAJ&pg=2633,5501632 |newspaper=Daytona Beach Morning Journal |date=15 April 1975 |access-date=28 January 2018}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |title=Sikkim Voters OK Merger With India |url=https://news.google.com/newspapers?nid=1755&dat=19750416&id=vDogAAAAIBAJ&pg=7319,25549 |newspaper=Sarasota Herald-Tribune |date=16 April 1975|access-date=28 January 2018}}</ref> From [[Sikkim]], India consolidated its control of the [[Chumbi Valley]], along with Bhutan.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Gupta |first=Shishir |date=30 October 2019 |title=China's early harvest plan on Sikkim border has no takers in India |url=https://www.hindustantimes.com/india-news/india-china-sr-talks-likely-to-be-held-in-next-3-months/story-4F8RpuP0bHVlfz05OI8egP.html |access-date=4 April 2024 |website=Hindustan Times}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Srivastava |first=Utkarsh |date=7 July 2017 |title=Sikkim standoff: Strategic importance of Siliguri corridor and why India should be wary of China |url=https://www.firstpost.com/india/sikkim-standoff-strategic-importance-of-siliguri-corridor-and-why-india-should-be-wary-of-china-3785967.html |access-date=4 April 2024 |website=Firstpost}}</ref>

==Geography==

The dimensions of the corridor are a matter of interpretation.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web|last=Singh|first=Lt Gen (Retd) KJ|author-link=Kamal Jit Singh|date=9 July 2017|title=India ready, theoretically: 'Threats' to Siliguri Corridor war-gamed|url=https://www.tribuneindia.com/news/archive/features/india-ready-theoretically-threats-to-siliguri-corridor-war-gamed-433789|access-date=2022-01-21|website=Tribune India}}</ref> Descriptions give it an area of {{convert|170 by 60|km|abbr=on}} with the narrowest section being {{convert|20-22|km|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /> [[Kamal Jit Singh]] places the length at {{convert|200|km|abbr=on}} with a width of {{convert|17 to 60|km|abbr=on}}, giving it an area of approximately {{convert|12200|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":2" />
Another description places its dimensions as approximately {{convert|200|km|abbr=on}} in length and {{convert|20 to 60|km|abbr=on}} wide, also giving it an area of approximately {{convert|12200|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web|last=Bhattacharya|first=Pinaki|date=2001|title=The Shiliguri Corridor: Question Mark on Security|url=https://www.satp.org/satporgtp/publication/faultlines/volume10/article7.htm|access-date=2022-01-21|publisher=South Asia Terrorism Portal}}</ref>

The corridor is located between Bangladesh to the southwest, Nepal on the northwest, and Bhutan to the north.<ref>Marcus Franda, "Bangladesh, The First Decades", South Asian Publishers Pvt. Ltd, New Delhi, 1982, p-126</ref> Between [[Sikkim]] and Bhutan lies the [[Chumbi Valley]] [[Tibet]]an territory.<ref name=":4">Partha S. Ghosh, "Cooperation and Conflict in South Asia", UPL, Dhaka, 1989, p-43</ref> The southern end of the Dolam plateau or Doklam tri boundary area slopes into the corridor.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Myers|first1=Steven Lee|last2=Barry|first2=Ellen|last3=Fisher|first3=Max|date=2017-07-26|title=How India and China Have Come to the Brink Over a Remote Mountain Pass|language=en-US|work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2017/07/26/world/asia/dolam-plateau-china-india-bhutan.html|access-date=2022-01-22|issn=0362-4331}}</ref> At the narrowest stretch, the corridor is generally formed by the [[Mechi River]] in the east; Nepal's [[Bhadrapur Municipality|Bhadrapur]] lies on the banks of the river.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Dixit|first=Kanak Mani|date=2002-08-01|title=Chicken's Neck|url=https://www.himalmag.com/chickens-neck/|access-date=2022-01-22|website=[[Himal Southasian]]}}</ref> Further north the [[Mechi Bridge]] connects [[Mechinagar Municipality|Mechinagar]].<ref>{{Cite news|last=|first=|date=2017-08-23|title=Approval of MoU between India & Nepal for laying down implementation arrangement for construction of new Bridge over Mechi River at Indo-Nepal border|work=Business Standard India|url=https://www.business-standard.com/article/news-cm/approval-of-mou-between-india-nepal-for-laying-down-implementation-arrangement-for-construction-of-new-bridge-over-mechi-river-at-indo-nepal-border-117082300967_1.html|access-date=2022-01-22}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=Khanal|first=Radha|date=26 November 2020|title=Asian Highway now connected with Nepal|url=https://theannapurnaexpress.com/news/asian-highway-now-connected-with-nepal-2888|access-date=2022-01-22|website=The Annapurna Express|language=en}}</ref>

==Vulnerability mitigation by India==
{{anchor | Mitigation }}

To mitigate the vulnerability India has undertaken several initiatives including enhancing security deployment as well as undertaking infrastructure development. To ensure seamless connectivity and military mobilization, India has prioritized developing high-capacity infrastructure in and around the corridor, notably under the Bharatmala Pariyojana. Projects include:<ref name="GKToday"/>

* Corridor Enhancement: Upgrading of major broad-gauge railway lines and highways to increase freight capacity and reduce transit time.<ref name="GKToday"/>
* Alternative Routes: Exploration and development of alternative connectivity solutions to reduce reliance on the single-access corridor, including the proposed Hili–Mahendraganj Corridor and the Kaladan Multimodal Transit Corridor through Myanmar.<ref name="Usthadian">{{cite web|title=Siliguri Corridor and India's Strategic Connectivity|website=Current Affairs Usthadian Academy|url=https://www.usthadian.com/siliguri-corridor-and-indias-strategic-connectivity/|date=3 August 2025}}</ref>
* Future Infrastructure: Consideration of subterranean infrastructure (tunnels) within the corridor to enhance security and reduce exposure to external threats.<ref name="GKToday"/>

===Connectivity===

India has embarked on a slew of projects. These include the construction of [[India-China Border Roads]], where, as of 2024, multiple phases of border roads have been constructed, and Advance Landing Grounds (ALGs), such as the Nyoma Advanced Landing Ground, which is nearing completion as of 2024.<ref>{{Cite news |date=29 July 2024 |title=Boost for security forces! Phase 3 of India-China border roads project started; to give impetus to road network in eastern Ladakh |url=https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/business/india-business/boost-for-security-forces-phase-3-of-india-china-border-roads-project-started-to-give-impetus-to-road-network-in-eastern-ladakh/articleshow/112100429.cms#:~:text=for%20security%20forces!-,Phase%203%20of%20India-China%20border%20roads%20project%20started;%20to,road%20network%20in%20eastern%20Ladakh&text=India's%20Border%20Roads%20Organisation,%20in,bolster%20infrastructure%20in%20eastern%20Ladakh. |access-date=5 April 2025 |work=The Times of India |issn=0971-8257}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Sharma |first=Shivani |date=2 November 2024 |title=India's highest airfield in Ladakh almost complete, to boost security near LAC |url=https://www.indiatoday.in/india/story/nyoma-advanced-landing-ground-highest-airfield-ladakh-boost-security-line-of-actual-control-china-2627129-2024-11-02 |access-date=5 April 2025 |website=India Today}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Pandit |first=Rajat |date=5 October 2024 |title=China outpacing India in technology, defence production & infrastructure, says IAF chief |url=https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/india/china-outpacing-india-in-technology-defence-production-infrastructure-says-iaf-chief/articleshow/113949580.cms# |access-date=5 April 2025 |work=The Times of India |issn=0971-8257}}</ref> Other projects include [[Ministry of Development of North Eastern Region#Northeast India connectivity projects|Northeastern India connectivity]] and [[India–Myanmar barrier#Look-East Connectivity|Look-East transnational connectivity]] projects including [[Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi-Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation|BIMSTEC]] and [[BBIN]] to create multiple alternatives to Silliguri corridor, including through Bangladesh and the sea.

All land transportation between the rest of India and its far northeastern states uses this corridor. The route has a major [[broad gauge]] railway line. Electrification of this double-track corridor is in progress with assistance from the [[Central Organization for Railway Electrification]] (CORE). Additionally, the old [[metre gauge]] line (recently converted to a {{convert|1.676|m}} broad-gauge line) connects [[Siliguri Junction railway station|Siliguri Junction]] with Islampur in the [[North Dinajpur district]] of West Bengal, via [[Bagdogra]] (the only airport of national interest in the corridor) and the bordering towns of Adhikari, [[Galgalia]], [[Thakurganj]], [[Naxalbari]] and Taiabpur with Nepal. As of 2024, to reduce dependence on the Siliguri Corridor, India is planning to build railway lines through [[Jogbani]] in [[Bihar]], which would enter [[Biratnagar]] in Nepal and then connect with [[New Mal Junction railway station|New Mal Junction]] in West Bengal.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Bhattacharya |first=Pinak Priya |date=16 July 2024 |title=India plans rail tracks through Nepal to bypass Chicken's Neck |url=https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/city/kolkata/india-plans-rail-tracks-through-nepal-to-bypass-chickens-neck/articleshow/111767186.cms |access-date=7 April 2025 |work=The Times of India |issn=0971-8257}}</ref> India is also developing railway lines through Bangladesh, which includes constructing new railway tracks and the [[Track gauge conversion|gauge conversion]] of existing railway tracks, in order to reduce dependence on the Siliguri Corridor, as of 2024.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Bhattacharya |first=Pinak Priya |date=16 June 2024 |title=New rail lines for Northeast via Bangladesh to bypass 'Chicken's Neck' |url=https://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/city/kolkata/new-rail-lines-for-northeast-via-bangladesh-to-bypass-chickens-neck/articleshow/111031488.cms |access-date=7 April 2025 |work=The Times of India |issn=0971-8257}}</ref>

As of 2024, there is no free-trade agreement between Bangladesh and India.<ref>{{Cite web |date=6 August 2024 |title=Bangladesh crisis: How trade with India could face major disruptions |url=https://www.indiatoday.in/business/story/bangladesh-crisis-sheikh-hasina-how-trade-with-india-could-face-major-disruptions-2577591-2024-08-06 |access-date=4 April 2025 |website=India Today}}</ref> The [[Tetulia Corridor]], an alternative to the Siliguri Corridor, is proposed under Article VIII of the India–Bangladesh Trade Agreement 1980, which states that "The two governments agree to make mutually beneficial arrangements for the use of their waterways, railways and roadways for commerce between the two countries and for passage of goods between two places in one country through the territory of the other".<ref name=":7">{{Cite news |last=Sarkar |first=Debasis |date=12 March 2019 |title=India to India Tetulia corridor to resurface as a major election issue |url=https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/elections/lok-sabha/india/india-to-india-tetulia-corridor-to-resurface-as-a-major-election-issue/articleshow/68367715.cms?from=mdr |access-date=4 April 2024 |work=The Economic Times |issn=0013-0389}}</ref> However, the opening of the [[Tetulia Corridor]] is still being discussed politically as of 2019.<ref name=":7" />

===Security===

The threat of a Chinese advance is still considered by Indian planners.<ref name=":5"/> A Chinese military advance of less than {{convert|130|km|abbr=on}} would cut off Bhutan, part of [[West Bengal]] and all of [[North-East India]], an area containing almost 50 million people. This situation arose during the [[Sino-Indian War|war between India and China]] in 1962.<ref name=":4" /> The security threat to this corridor was heightened during the [[2017 China–India border standoff|2017 Doklam incident]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=Singh|first=D. K.|date=2018-08-11|title=This is the first official account of the India-China face-off in Doklam|url=https://theprint.in/defence/this-is-the-first-official-account-of-the-india-china-face-off-in-doklam/97014/|access-date=2022-01-21|website=ThePrint}}</ref> The probability of China cutting off seven states in northeast India has been questioned.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Asthana|first=Alok|date=1 August 2017|title=Does It Make Military Sense for India to Mount the Barricades at Doklam?|url=https://thewire.in/diplomacy/india-china-war-consequences-doklam|access-date=2022-01-22|website=The Wire}}</ref> The Indian Army has said that the Siliguri Corridor is the Army's strongest defensive line.<ref name=":6" />

'''Military Bases and Deployment''': India has several forces stationed on the borders, the [[Indian Army]] and [[Indo-Tibetan Border Police]] man the border with China; [[Sashastra Seema Bal]] is deployed along the border with Nepal and Bhutan and the [[Border Security Force]] for Bangladesh.<ref name=":2" /> The strip is also patrolled by the [[Indian Army]], the [[Assam Rifles]], and state police forces including the [[West Bengal Police]].<ref name=":2" /> The security threat posed by the corridor decreased following the creation of Bangladesh in 1971.<ref name=":5">{{Cite news|last=Joshi|first=Manoj|author-link=Manoj Joshi (journalist)|date=10 July 2017|title=Chink In The Checker's Board|work=ORF/ Outlook|url=https://www.orfonline.org/research/doklam-chink-checker-board/#:~:text=The%20Chinese%20action%20in%20the,of%20aggression%2C%20presenting%20fait%20accompli.&text=The%20location%20is%20near%20the,proximate%20to%20Bhutan%20and%20China.|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20181003131536/https://www.outlookindia.com/magazine/story/chink-in-the-checkers-board/299090|archive-date=3 October 2018}}</ref> Internal threats to the corridor are numerous.<ref name=":5"/> Militant groups known to have used the corridor include the [[United Liberation Front of Asom|United Liberation Front of Assam]] (ULFA) and the [[National Socialist Council of Nagaland]] (NSCN).<ref name=":3" />

* Advanced defensive assets are already stationed in the area also includes [[Anti-aircraft warfare|air defense systems]] (such as [[S-400 missile system|S-400)]], a [[BrahMos]] missile [[regiment]] and a [[Squadron (aviation)|squadron]] of [[Dassault Rafale|Rafale fighter aircraft]] of the Indian Air Force's Rafale fighter aircraft based at [[Hasimara Air Force Station]].<ref name=":6">{{Cite web |last=Sharma |first=Shivani |date=4 April 2025 |editor-last=Jha |editor-first=Anuja |title=India fortifies 'Chicken's Neck' as Bangladesh, China eye strategic corridor |url=https://www.indiatoday.in/india/story/india-fortifies-chicken-neck-as-bangladesh-china-eye-strategic-corridor-2703661-2025-04-04 |access-date=4 April 2025 |website=India Today}}</ref><ref name="RakshaAnirveda">{{cite web|title=Shielding the Eastern Frontier: India Strengthens Siliguri Corridor, Establishes Three New Garrisons|website=Raksha Anirveda|url=https://raksha-anirveda.com/shielding-the-eastern-frontier-india-strengthens-siliguri-corridor-establishes-three-new-garrisons/|date=14 November 2025}}</ref>
* The corridor and the surrounding region are secured by multiple military formations, primarily Indian Army's Trishakti [[Corps]] ([[XXXIII Corps (India)|XXXIII Corps]]),  which guards the corridor and Sikkim.<ref name=":6" /><ref name="Hindu">{{cite web|last=Trivedi|first=Saurabh|title=India strengthens eastern defences with three new garrisons near Siliguri 'Chicken's Neck'|website=The Hindu|url=https://www.thehindu.com/news/national/assam/india-strengthens-eastern-defences-with-three-new-garrisons-near-siliguri-chickens-neck/article70287683.ece|date=16 November 2025}}</ref> In a significant move to reinforce the eastern frontier, India established three new military garrisons in late 2025 to boost surveillance, readiness, and coordination with the Border Security Force (BSF):<ref name="Hindu"/>
** Lachit Borphukan Military Station at Bamuni (near Dhubri, Assam). This is the first major Army base near the Bangladesh border in Assam.<ref name="ETGarrisons">{{cite web|title=India sets up three new military garrisons along Bangladesh border to fortify 'Chicken's Neck'|website=The Economic Times|url=https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/news/defence/india-sets-up-three-new-military-garrisons-along-bangladesh-border-to-fortify-chickens-neck/articleshow/100000000.cms|date=8 November 2025}}</ref>
** Forward Bases at Kishanganj (Bihar) and Chopra (West Bengal), which are intended for rapid troop deployment and intelligence coordination.<ref name="EurasiaReview"/>

==In popular culture==

[[Humphrey Hawksley]], in his 2000 novel ''[[Dragon Fire (novel)|Dragon Fire]],'' briefly authors a situation where China cuts off India's land route to its northeastern territories.<ref name=":3"/> ''Assassin's Mace'' (2011) by Brigadier Bob Butalia also involves such a situation involving [[Doklam]] and [[Jaldhaka River]].<ref name=":2" />

==See also==

* [[Chicken's neck (disambiguation)|Chicken's neck]], disambiguation
* [[Suwałki Gap]]

==Notes==
{{Reflist}}

;Bibliography

Border-Effect and the 'Untimely' City in North Bengal|title=Logistical Asia: The Labour of Making a World Region|publisher=Palgrave Macmillan, Springer|year=2018|isbn=9789811083334|editor-last=Neilson|editor-first=Brett|lccn=2018935185|ref={{sfnref|Atig Ghosh, The Importance of Being Siliguri|2018}}|editor-last2=Rossiter|editor-first2=Ned|editor-last3=Samaddar|editor-first3=Ranabir}}
