contested.title: Michael Woroniecki
contested.raw_text:
{{Short description|Christian missionary (born 1954)}} {{About|the street preacher|the Christian evangelist and comedian|Mike Warnke}} {{Multiple issues| {{Cleanup|reason=lots of verb-tense issues, out-of-date time periods, etc|date=January 2026}} {{POV|date=January 2026}} {{BLP sources|date=January 2026}} {{Primary sources|date=January 2026}} }} {{Use mdy dates|date=January 2019}} {{Infobox clergy | name = Michael Woroniecki | image =Street Preacher DNC Aug 28 2008.jpg | caption = Woroniecki at the 2008 [[Democratic National Convention]]. | birth_date ={{Birth date and age|1954|02|04}} | birth_place = [[Grand Rapids, Michigan]], US | death_date = | death_place = | church = Current: None Former: Cornerstone Christian Fellowship | other_names = Michael Warnecki, Warneki, Worneki, Mike War | education = [[Central Michigan University]]
[[Melodyland School of Theology]]
[[Fuller Theological Seminary]]
[[Aquinas College (Michigan)|Aquinas College]] }} '''Michael Peter Woroniecki''' (also '''Michael Warnecki''', '''Warneki''', '''Worneki''', '''Mike War''', and '''Mike Wazowski''';Suzy Spencer, Breaking Point, St. Martin's Library, p. 176,213 (Warnecki, Worneki "and a few other names") born February 4, 1954) is an American evangelical [[street preacher]] who has been described as a [[cult]] leader.{{cite AV media|people=Hakami, Elli (director), Hobbs, Julian P. (director)|date=2026|title=The Cult Behind the Killer: The Andrea Yates Story|type=television series|language=English|publisher=HBO Max|access-date=January 7, 2026|url=https://www.hbomax.com/shows/cult-behind-the-killer-the-andrea-yates-story/d6475c20-a627-4cdc-85a0-cb38d98aafd1}} Woroniecki is well known for his ministry on college campuses and at various public events across the US over the span of the last 30 years.{{cite magazine|url=http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,1001706,00.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20060923002641/http://www.time.com/time/magazine/article/0,9171,1001706,00.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=September 23, 2006|title=The Yates Odyssey|last=Berryman|first=Anne|author2=Fowler, Deborah |author3=Hylton, Hilary |author4= Fulton, Greg |date=July 26, 2006|magazine=[[Time (magazine)|Time]]|accessdate=March 10, 2010}}{{Cite news|url=http://a.abcnews.com/Primetime/Story?id=2244865&page=1|title=Insanity Verdict|last=Cuomo|first=Chris|date=July 27, 2006|work=Primetime|publisher=ABC News|accessdate=March 10, 2010}}{{Cite news|url=https://abcnews.go.com/GMA/story?id=126222&page=1|title=I Shared Jesus With Them|date=March 27, 2002|work=Good Morning America|publisher=ABC News|accessdate=March 10, 2010|location=New York}} ==Early life and family== Woroniecki was the youngest of a large Polish [[Roman Catholic Church|Catholic]] family who was raised in the city of [[Grand Rapids, Michigan]]. His mother became involved in the [[Catholic Charismatic Movement]] in the early 1970s and sought to introduce her children to the [[Born again Christianity|born again]] experience. In 1972, seeking a way out of Grand Rapids, he "made a deal with God" that he would attend prayer meetings with his mother if he could make All-City [[Halfback (American football)|tailback]] in football and thus receive a scholarship for college. He got the title and the scholarship.{{Cite book|last=O'Malley|first=Suzanne|title="Are you there alone?" The unspeakable Crime of Andrea Yates|year=2004|publisher=Simon & Schuster|location=New York|pages=101|isbn=978-0-7432-4485-5}}{{Cite web|url=http://www.michaelpeterworoniecki.com/2010/01/michael-woroniecki-how-to-win-crazy-war.html|title=Michael Woroniecki "How To Win The Crazy War"|last=Woroniecki|first=Michael|accessdate=March 11, 2010}} Woroniecki attended [[Central Michigan University]] (CMU) where he studied psychology and played varsity football from 1972 to 1976. He boasted of being able to [[bench press]] 400 lbs and run a 4.5 second [[40-yard dash]]. Woroniecki explains in his Christian testimony that he forgot his deal with God and had a "wild streak", involving himself in alcohol and partying. Woroniecki states that he was known to his teammates as the "Crazy War" and says he was arrested the summer of his freshman year for fighting in a bar, just to prove to his peers how tough he was.{{Citation|last=Woroniecki|first=Michael|title=The Victory In Defeat|year=1994|url=http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/VictoryinDefeat.pdf|url-status=dead|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20160304041118/http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/VictoryinDefeat.pdf|archivedate=March 4, 2016}} During spring football practice in 1974, Woroniecki suffered a disabling football injury that threatened his athletic aspirations. About the same time, Woroniecki's mother gave him a [[Bible]], which he began to read. Woroniecki says God used this time of suffering and depression to break him of his pride, preparing him to receive the [[Gospel]].O'Malley, p. 102. Woroniecki attended the annual Catholic Charismatic Conference at [[University of Notre Dame]] the weekend of June 14, 1974 with his family. He was in the stadium when he says he told God that he didn't know what the saying "born again" meant, but that he wanted everything that the Lord had for him. While sitting alone in the stadium, Woroniecki says that he gave his life to Jesus. At that moment, Woroniecki believes that he "met the living Jesus". "The grass and goalposts were the same but I was changed" says Woroniecki, adding that he found significance in the recently painted ''[[Word of Life (mural)|Touchdown Jesus]]'' on the library mural right in front of him. Woroniecki returned to college football practice in July that same summer. One day after practice, while in a bar with his teammates, Woroniecki says he ordered water in the place of his usual beer. His friends becoming curious, Woroniecki explained that he had met Jesus. Used to his outrageous life-of-the-party humor, they all mistakenly thought he was joking, and Woroniecki became the focus of ridicule and rejection by his teammates. He writes that he could not understand why people like [[Roger Staubach]], a famous Christian athlete, were respected, yet he was rejected, until he read John 12:24 and 25. He reasoned that if the world hated Jesus without cause, they would also hate and reject him if he followed him. The same year, Woroniecki and his teammates went on to win the [[NCAA Division II Football Championship]] for the only time in Central Michigan's history.O'Malley, p. 103. Woroniecki graduated from Central Michigan with a [[Bachelor of Science|B.S.]] in [[Behavioral Sciences]] in 1976. While at CMU, Woroniecki met a [[cheerleader]] from [[Detroit|Detroit, Michigan]], Leslie Jean Ochalek (later renamed "Rachel Rebekah"). Woroniecki and Ochalek married in 1979. ==Education== Charles and Rose Woroniecki, Michael Woroniecki's parents, were members of the [[Basilica of St. Adalbert (Grand Rapids, Michigan)|Basilica of Saint Adalbert]], a Roman Catholic church in the Polish west side of Grand Rapids. Michael Woroniecki attended a Catholic grade school adjoining his family's [[parish]] and then advanced to [[West Catholic High School]], another [[parochial school]] in Grand Rapids. During his senior year of high school in 1972, Woroniecki began attending Catholic Charismatic prayer meetings, part of his "deal with God." After graduating from Central Michigan, Woroniecki attended [[Melodyland Christian Center#School of Theology|Melodyland School of Theology]] at [[Anaheim, California]] starting in 1976. A month after returning home from seminary that summer, his mother died from colon [[cancer]]. Woroniecki applied to the [[Dominican Order|Dominican]] and [[Franciscan]] Orders of the Catholic Church with the intention of helping to bring reform from within the Church. In his final round of interviews with the Dominican seminary near [[Chicago, Illinois]], Woroniecki was refused immediate admission and decided on a different course of action. He then applied to the Franciscan seminary in his own hometown of Grand Rapids. After a semester of study at [[Aquinas College (Michigan)|Aquinas College]], Woroniecki was again offered an extended preparatory period. He chose to pursue other means of study. In 1978, Woroniecki was accepted at [[Fuller Theological Seminary]] in [[Pasadena, California]], a non-denominational institution. Woroniecki says he was "deeply grieved" by the professionalism and business-like attitude towards the things of God at Fuller. This atmosphere, Woroniecki claims, ultimately compelled him to stand on the campus lawn and preach "the living Jesus" to fellow students and professors when they came out of the chapel. He confronted classmates over the attitudes of scholastic pride and hypocrisy that he thought to contradict the life of Jesus of Nazareth.O'Malley, p. 104. Woroniecki obtained his Master of Divinity degree from Fuller in 1980. Upon returning to Grand Rapids, Woroniecki briefly attended and occasionally preached at [[Maranatha Campus Ministries|Maranatha Church]]. Shortly thereafter, Woroniecki left to start his own home church and street preaching ministry.O'Malley, p. 105. ==Ministry== ===Preaching=== Woroniecki returned to Grand Rapids after graduating from Fuller Seminary in the summer of 1980, where he preached on the streets and at various events while carrying a sign or cross, starting his own ministry called Cornerstone Christian Fellowship. As a result of, according to Woroniecki, "youthful zeal" and a city unsure of how to handle his unconventional evangelistic approach, Woroniecki was arrested numerous times over the next year and a half.Evangelist Michael Woroniecki Meets the Press in the 'City of Churches, ''Curt Christy, December 1, 1981'' In October 1981, he offered to leave town if the pending charges against him would be dropped.Street Preacher Will Leave City to Beat Charges, ''Steve Grinczel, The Grand Rapids Press, October 8, 1981''Steve Grinczel,"Woman Prefers to See Street Preacher Tried", Grand Rapids Press, October 12, 1981Assistant City Attorney Michael D. McGuire, "Dissent!: Woroniecki Case, Grand Rapids Press, October 18, 1981 The offer was accepted and Woroniecki began to travel the US, stopping first in [[Atlanta, Georgia]] where street preaching is permitted.Follow Up: High Volume Street Preacher Reports Progress in Sunbelt, Grand Rapids Press, December 14, 1981"Traveling Preacher Arrested," Arizona State University ''State Press'', November 3, 1986.Jeanette Waite, "Anti-Mormon Protest Disturbs Campus", BYU Press, October 6, 1994 [http://newsnet.byu.edu/story.cfm/37644 Article] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040617152615/http://newsnet.byu.edu/story.cfm/37644 |date=June 17, 2004 }} To deliver his message, Woroniecki chooses college campuses, large sporting and political events as well as city centers. His wife and six adult children are often seen ministering alongside him.The Truth About Michael Woroniecki [http://www.michaelworoniecki.blogspot.com "Website"] {{Webarchive|url=https://archive.today/20121127070112/http://www.michaelworoniecki.blogspot.com/ |date=November 27, 2012 }} In the early 1990s, he shipped a Ford van to [[England]] and then ferried it to France. The family traveled together across Europe and then to Tangier, [[Morocco]]. In May 1995, they preached in [[Casablanca]], where Muslim law makes it a crime to declare Jesus as the Son of God. A riot resulted and the family was arrested. They were interrogated for eight hours by state officials, then ordered to leave the country.O'Malley, p. 106; Seattle Times News Services, Around the World--Rabat, Morocco, "Oregon Missionary Family Arrested in Casablanca", The Seattle Times, May 31, 1995; "Casablanca {{sic|?|Chrisitians|expected=Christians}}", USA Today (European Edition), approx. May 31 – June 2, 1995, p. 2. Since leaving Grand Rapids 30 years ago, Woroniecki and his family have preached in all 50 U.S. states, [[Latin America]], [[Europe]], [[Russia]] and [[Morocco]].[http://www.kaleo.org/home/index.cfm?event=displayArticle&ustory_id=dee202ef-d301-4a94-a4f9-cdb74541740a Article], Judgment Day? Freedom of Speech on Campus, John Nakatsu, Kaleo (The Voice), March 19, 2008Going for the Gold Steve; Grand Rapids Exile Preaches on at Olympics, ''Steve Grinczel, The Grand Rapids Press, August 4, 1996'' ===Music=== Michael Woroniecki began creating music with an electronic keyboard in 1986.Michael and David Woroniecki Bio on Reverb Nation.com [http://www.reverbnation.com/michaelanddavidworoniecki#/page_object/page_object_bio/artist_656935 "Site"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121019215059/http://www.reverbnation.com/michaelanddavidworoniecki |date=October 19, 2012 }} In the early 1990s he began using this music as another form of ministry on the streets of [[Europe]]. During the summers of 1992 to 1996 he performed in downtown city plazas and centers in places like [[London]], [[Paris]], [[Barcelona]], [[Lisbon]], [[Rome]], [[Berlin]], [[Budapest]], [[Athens]], and [[Moscow]]. Woroniecki says he used the music along with dances and Biblical skits as a means of communicating the gospel in countries where they did not speak the language. Michael does not consider himself professional but believes "that music is a powerful tool to touch people of all walks of life". During the 1996 Atlanta [[Summer Olympic Games]], Woroniecki was reported to have performed with his family outside the various venues using the event to reach the international crowds with his message. Beginning in 1997, Woroniecki traveled throughout [[Mexico]] and [[Latin America]], playing on the streets. Woroniecki claims that, from 2006 to 2009, he has self-produced eight music CDs which he and his family have distributed in their travels.Michael and David Woroniecki on Reverb Nation [http://www.reverbnation.com/michaelanddavidworoniecki "Website"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121019215059/http://www.reverbnation.com/michaelanddavidworoniecki |date=October 19, 2012 }}Music by Michael Woroniecki: Time Seizure-''2002-English'', Traveline: ¿Donde Puedes Ir?-''2003-Spanish / English'', Measures of a New World-''2008-English'', Las Alturas de los Quebrantados-''2009-Spanish / English '', Mi Amigo-''2009-Instrumental'', Aperture, 2009-InstrumentalVaried Followers share Faith with Campus, ''The BG News (Bowling Green), Stephanie Guigou, September 25, 2008'' [http://issuu.com/bgviews/docs/bgnews092508 "Archive"] ==Religious views== [[File:WoronieckiwithsigninTimesSquare.jpg|thumb|left|x200px|alt=Woroniecki carries sign in Times Square that reads "Discover forgiveness for all your sin and eternal life in the Living Jesus".|Woroniecki in Times Square, 2008.]] Woroniecki preaches that one must "get alone, study the [[New Testament]] and seek the Living [[Jesus]] to be Born Again".God's Warriors come to UCO, ''Aaron Wright, The Vista, University of Central OK., August 28, 2007'' [http://www.thevistaonline.com/2.8883/god-s-warriors-come-to-uco-1.1154838 "Archive"]{{dead link|date=December 2017 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} He says that today's "churches" are not the true Church as seen in the Scriptures and cannot save anyone.Spencer, p. 137. Woroniecki has used large banner signs and often a wooden cross to convey his message for the past 35 years.Michael Woroniecki preaches Jesus not murder, ''Central Michigan Life, Heather Bellife, May 22, 2002'' [http://www.cm-life.com/2002/05/22/michaelworonieckipreachesjesusnotmurder "Archive"] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100303012653/http://www.cm-life.com/2002/05/22/michaelworonieckipreachesjesusnotmurder/ |date=March 3, 2010 }} In his ministry Woroniecki addresses the Biblical themes of hypocrisy in the institutional system, [[Grace (Christianity)|grace]], [[salvation]], forgiveness, [[hell]], [[judgment]], deception and the consequences of sin. Because of this, Woroniecki has taken his share of verbal and physical opposition. He claims that these are the "last days" and that Jesus will soon return to judge the world.Interview with Woroniecki family member outside the Republican National Convention, Baron Dave Romm, KFAI-News FM 90.3 and 106.7, Minneapolis-St. Paul, MN, September 3, 2008 [http://www.romm.org/audio/20080903rnc28joshua.mp3]Explosion, ''Michael Woroniecki, Tract, circa 2003''[http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Xplosion.pdf "Archive"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924012734/http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Xplosion.pdf |date=September 24, 2015 }} Woroniecki cites various Scripture verses in his pamphlets to substantiate his message that only by faith can a man be saved from hell and reconciled to God, a doctrine accepted by most mainstream Christians and defined by Martin Luther as [[sola fide]].Fact or Fiction, ''Michael Woroniecki, Tract, circa 2002''[http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Fact%20or%20Fiction.pdf "Archive"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924012731/http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Fact%20or%20Fiction.pdf |date=September 24, 2015 }}What About YOU?, ''Michael Woroniecki, Tract, circa 2003''[http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/What%20About%20YOU.pdf "Archive"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924012733/http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/What%20About%20YOU.pdf |date=September 24, 2015 }}Slammed, ''Michael Woroniecki, Tract, circa 2004'' [http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Slammed.pdf "Archive"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924012732/http://www.fileden.com/files/2010/1/30/2744045/Slammed.pdf |date=September 24, 2015 }} The Bible figures strongly in Woroniecki's beliefs and is a focal point of his preaching. Despite not holding a conventional position in an established religion, Woroniecki's knowledge of Scripture and theology is extensive.; William Holm, "A Prophet Without Honor", Grand Rapids Magazine, February 1981 His years of personal and scholastic study at three seminaries are reflected in his writings. Woroniecki believes in [[Biblical inerrancy]] and considers the Scriptures authoritative on all matters of life. Woroniecki's message is met on the streets with a variety of responses.Jerry Morlock, "Disturbing Word Jails Evangelist", Grand Rapids Press, September 14, 1980Spencer, pp 177,178.Steve Grinczel, "Woman Prefers to See Street Preacher Tried", Grand Rapids Press, October 12, 1981 ; Stuart Villanueva, "Religious Conflict", The Battalion, Texas A&M University, April 26, 2001 ==Andrea Yates== Woroniecki first met Rusty Yates in the mid-1980s while preaching on the campus of [[Auburn University]]. Rusty took some literature, and they corresponded by mail for the next several years. In 1989 Rusty introduced the Woronieckis to [[Andrea Yates|Andrea Kennedy]], whom he had recently begun dating.O'Malley, p. 28. Woroniecki said of this meeting that Andrea seemed slightly intimidated, and that he advised them not to marry quickly, the same advice he gave to other young couples.O'Malley, p. 29. The Yates and Woroniecki families stayed in touch by letter and telephone, and in 1998, the Yates family traveled to Miami after hearing that the Woronieckis were selling their motor home.O'Malley, p. 31. Rusty bought the bus, a 1978 GMC that Woroniecki had used for his family's travels. During the week that the Yates stayed, Woroniecki confronted Rusty, saying that he was "willing to sacrifice his wife and children for the sake of his job".{{Cite news|title=A preacher speaks out|date=March 20, 2002|work=Dateline NBC|publisher=NBC News}}{{cite news|title=Unrepentant in Yates tragedy|last=Nichols|first=Bruce|date=April 20, 2002|newspaper=The Toronto Star}} After this confrontation, Rusty and Woroniecki "grew apart". In the months leading up to the drownings, Rusty described the relationship with the Woronieckis as not being very close. Andrea Yates had suffered mental health difficulties for several years leading up to 2001. In June 1999 Andrea took an overdose of sedatives and was hospitalized several times over the next two years. She was prescribed a variety of antidepressant and antipsychotic medications. On June 20, 2001, a few weeks after being discharged from Devereaux Texas Treatment Center, Andrea killed all five of her children.O'Malley, chapters 2 & 3 In early 2002 Michael Woroniecki came to the attention of the national media when Yates's trial started. Yates's defense attorney, George Parnham, placed a copy of Woroniecki's newsletter ''The Perilous Times'' into evidence,{{Cite news|title=Examining a spiritual leader's influence|last=Gesalman|first=Anne Belli|date=March 18, 2002|work=Newsweek|pages=8}} suggesting that the material was dangerous for someone like Yates,O'Malley, p. 175. and defense team psychiatrist Lucy Puryear said on ''[[Good Morning America]]'' that "Yates's fate may have been different if she never met Woroniecki". Certain media outlets alleged that Woroniecki bore some responsibility for influencing Andrea.O'Malley, p. 109. However, both Michael Woroniecki and Rusty Yates dismissed the allegations. According to the ''[[Houston Chronicle]]'', "[Russell] Yates said his relationship with Woroniecki was more like learning from a book. He was free to accept what he wanted and reject that which he didn't".Duke Journal of Gender Law and Policy, ''Volume 10:1, 2003 Appendix 1'', Timeline of Andrea Yates Life and Trial [http://www.law.duke.edu/journals/djglp/downloads/gen10p61.pdf "Archive"] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120305205404/http://www.law.duke.edu/journals/djglp/downloads/gen10p61.pdf |date=March 5, 2012 }} Yates said "That's just crazy" when asked whether or not his wife was influenced by Woroniecki.Lost in the message? Cleric says he's not to blame for Yates demons, ''Lisa Teachy, The Houston Chronicle, April 6, 2002'' [http://www.chron.com/CDA/archives/archive.mpl?id=2002_3533886 "Archive"] He stated that Andrea had suffered schizophrenia, depression and delusions for years. Both Yates and Woroniecki characterized the connection between Woroniecki's teachings and Andrea Yates's state of mind as nothing more than [[sensationalism|sensationalist reporting]]. Woroniecki "... certainly didn't cause the delusion," says Yates. Woroniecki called the defense's claims "ridiculous". He stated that he had only met Andrea personally four or five times over 20 years and that his relationship with her was "one of nothing but love and compassion". Woroniecki said that he had warned Russell Yates that Andrea and their children needed his love.[They Needed Jesus, ''Steve Grinczel, The Grand Rapids Press, March 9, 2002''] [[Peter Jennings]] of ''[[World News with Diane Sawyer|World News Tonight]]'' reported that Rachel Woroniecki wrote to Rusty and Andrea that they needed to reconcile their marriage.Did the Preacher see it coming?, ''World News Tonight, Peter Jennings, January 21, 2002'' In an interview with ''[[Dateline NBC]]'' in March 2002, Mrs. Woroniecki said:
"A major problem is that people think that by emulating our lifestyle they can have the joy and the love that we have without building a foundation in Jesus Christ."
Woroniecki was quoted by the ''[[Grand Rapids Press]]'' as saying,
"I will gladly sacrifice my reputation if it can spare Andrea from the death penalty and give her a second chance at life. However ... it is deceitful and irresponsible to blame doctors, hospitals, clinics or 'postpartum depression.' They (Rusty and Andrea Yates) both know that the issues which culminated in this tragedy are much, much deeper."
==Currently== Woroniecki and family members carried signs and preached outside both the [[Democratic National Convention]] in [[Denver]] and the [[Republican National Convention]] in [[Saint Paul, Minnesota|Saint Paul]] in 2008.[http://www.mlive.com/news/grand-rapids/index.ssf/2008/09/street_preacher_family_undaunt.html Street preacher, family undaunted by jeers] The Grand Rapids Press, September 23, 2008 In November 2010, Woroniecki and his family were seen ministering in Times Square in New York City, promoting their website www.ifanyoneisthirsty.com. The author writes that the primary point of their message was "that Jesus is alive and that we need to actively seek him out", adding that despite any controversy, the family seemed to him to be genuine in their efforts to "go all over the world and celebrate life and the living Jesus."[http://voices.yahoo.com/woroniecki-family-times-square-7122832.html?cat=34 Woroniecki Family in Times Square] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140518061214/http://voices.yahoo.com/woroniecki-family-times-square-7122832.html?cat=34 |date=May 18, 2014 }} by Jesse Schmitt, November 8, 2010 Jack Wellman, Christian evangelist, author and regular contributor to ''OVI Magazine'' and ''[[Yahoo! Voices]]'', confirms that Woroniecki continues to take his message "to the world and the streets of the nations". Woroniecki's current message, according to Wellman, taken from Woroniecki's website and literature, is:
"Are you willing to look deeper, beyond it all? Please give us a chance to help you. It will be well worth your time. What we offer you is not a new group, "church", formulas, self exaltation or doctrines. What we offer you is the love of the living Jesus who has the answers that you are looking for".[http://www.ovimagazine.com/art/6491 Is Anyone Thirsty? The Woroniecki Family's Story] by Jack Wellman, Nov. 17, 2010[http://www.ifanyoneisthirsty.com If Anyone Is Thirsty ] Michael Woroniecki's Website

contested.clean_text: {{Multiple issues| }} {{Infobox clergy | name = Michael Woroniecki | image =Street Preacher DNC Aug 28 2008.jpg | caption = Woroniecki at the 2008 Democratic National Convention. | birth_date = | birth_place = Grand Rapids, Michigan, US | death_date = | death_place = | church = Current: None Former: Cornerstone Christian Fellowship | other_names = Michael Warnecki, Warneki, Worneki, Mike War | education = Central Michigan UniversityMelodyland School of TheologyFuller Theological SeminaryAquinas College }} Michael Peter Woroniecki (also Michael Warnecki, Warneki, Worneki, Mike War, and Mike Wazowski; born February 4, 1954) is an American evangelical street preacher who has been described as a cult leader. Woroniecki is well known for his ministry on college campuses and at various public events across the US over the span of the last 30 years. Woroniecki was the youngest of a large Polish Catholic family who was raised in the city of Grand Rapids, Michigan. His mother became involved in the Catholic Charismatic Movement in the early 1970s and sought to introduce her children to the born again experience. In 1972, seeking a way out of Grand Rapids, he "made a deal with God" that he would attend prayer meetings with his mother if he could make All-City tailback in football and thus receive a scholarship for college. He got the title and the scholarship. Woroniecki attended Central Michigan University (CMU) where he studied psychology and played varsity football from 1972 to 1976. He boasted of being able to bench press 400 lbs and run a 4.5 second 40-yard dash. During spring football practice in 1974, Woroniecki suffered a disabling football injury that threatened his athletic aspirations. About the same time, Woroniecki's mother gave him a Bible, which he began to read. Woroniecki says God used this time of suffering and depression to break him of his pride, preparing him to receive the Gospel. Woroniecki attended the annual Catholic Charismatic Conference at University of Notre Dame the weekend of June 14, 1974 with his family. He was in the stadium when he says he told God that he didn't know what the saying "born again" meant, but that he wanted everything that the Lord had for him. While sitting alone in the stadium, Woroniecki says that he gave his life to Jesus. At that moment, Woroniecki believes that he "met the living Jesus". "The grass and goalposts were the same but I was changed" says Woroniecki, adding that he found significance in the recently painted Touchdown Jesus on the library mural right in front of him. Woroniecki graduated from Central Michigan with a B.S. in Behavioral Sciences in 1976. While at CMU, Woroniecki met a cheerleader from Detroit, Michigan, Leslie Jean Ochalek (later renamed "Rachel Rebekah"). Woroniecki and Ochalek married in 1979. Woroniecki obtained his Master of Divinity degree from Fuller in 1980. Woroniecki returned to Grand Rapids after graduating from Fuller Seminary in the summer of 1980, where he preached on the streets and at various events while carrying a sign or cross, starting his own ministry called Cornerstone Christian Fellowship. In October 1981, he offered to leave town if the pending charges against him would be dropped. The offer was accepted and Woroniecki began to travel the US, stopping first in Atlanta, Georgia where street preaching is permitted. To deliver his message, Woroniecki chooses college campuses, large sporting and political events as well as city centers. His wife and six adult children are often seen ministering alongside him. In the early 1990s, he shipped a Ford van to England and then ferried it to France. The family traveled together across Europe and then to Tangier, Morocco. In May 1995, they preached in Casablanca, where Muslim law makes it a crime to declare Jesus as the Son of God. A riot resulted and the family was arrested. They were interrogated for eight hours by state officials, then ordered to leave the country. Since leaving Grand Rapids 30 years ago, Woroniecki and his family have preached in all 50 U.S. states, Latin America, Europe, Russia and Morocco. Michael Woroniecki began creating music with an electronic keyboard in 1986. In the early 1990s he began using this music as another form of ministry on the streets of Europe. During the summers of 1992 to 1996 he performed in downtown city plazas and centers in places like London, Paris, Barcelona, Lisbon, Rome, Berlin, Budapest, Athens, and Moscow. thumb|left|x200px|alt=Woroniecki carries sign in Times Square that reads "Discover forgiveness for all your sin and eternal life in the Living Jesus".|Woroniecki in Times Square, 2008. Woroniecki preaches that one must "get alone, study the New Testament and seek the Living Jesus to be Born Again". He says that today's "churches" are not the true Church as seen in the Scriptures and cannot save anyone. Woroniecki has used large banner signs and often a wooden cross to convey his message for the past 35 years. Woroniecki cites various Scripture verses in his pamphlets to substantiate his message that only by faith can a man be saved from hell and reconciled to God, a doctrine accepted by most mainstream Christians and defined by Martin Luther as sola fide. The Bible figures strongly in Woroniecki's beliefs and is a focal point of his preaching. Despite not holding a conventional position in an established religion, Woroniecki's knowledge of Scripture and theology is extensive. His years of personal and scholastic study at three seminaries are reflected in his writings. Woroniecki first met Rusty Yates in the mid-1980s while preaching on the campus of Auburn University. Rusty took some literature, and they corresponded by mail for the next several years. In 1989 Rusty introduced the Woronieckis to Andrea Kennedy, whom he had recently begun dating. Woroniecki said of this meeting that Andrea seemed slightly intimidated, and that he advised them not to marry quickly, the same advice he gave to other young couples. The Yates and Woroniecki families stayed in touch by letter and telephone, and in 1998, the Yates family traveled to Miami after hearing that the Woronieckis were selling their motor home. Rusty bought the bus, a 1978 GMC that Woroniecki had used for his family's travels. After this confrontation, Rusty and Woroniecki "grew apart". In early 2002 Michael Woroniecki came to the attention of the national media suggesting that the material was dangerous for someone like Yates, and defense team psychiatrist Lucy Puryear said on Good Morning America that "Yates's fate may have been different if she never met Woroniecki". However, both Michael Woroniecki and Rusty Yates dismissed the allegations. According to the Houston Chronicle, "[Russell] Yates said his relationship with Woroniecki was more like learning from a book. He was free to accept what he wanted and reject that which he didn't". Yates said "That's just crazy" when asked whether or not his wife was influenced by Woroniecki. He stated that Andrea had suffered schizophrenia, depression and delusions for years. Peter Jennings of World News Tonight reported that Rachel Woroniecki wrote to Rusty and Andrea that they needed to reconcile their marriage. In an interview with Dateline NBC in March 2002, Mrs. Woroniecki said: "A major problem is that people think that by emulating our lifestyle they can have the joy and the love that we have without building a foundation in Jesus Christ." Woroniecki was quoted by the Grand Rapids Press as saying, "I will gladly sacrifice my reputation if it can spare Andrea from the death penalty and give her a second chance at life. However ... it is deceitful and irresponsible to blame doctors, hospitals, clinics or 'postpartum depression.' They (Rusty and Andrea Yates) both know that the issues which culminated in this tragedy are much, much deeper." In November 2010, Woroniecki and his family were seen ministering in Times Square in New York City, promoting their website www.ifanyoneisthirsty.com. The author writes that the primary point of their message was "that Jesus is alive and that we need to actively seek him out", adding that despite any controversy, the family seemed to him to be genuine in their efforts to "go all over the world and celebrate life and the living Jesus." Jack Wellman, Christian evangelist, author and regular contributor to OVI Magazine and Yahoo! Voices, confirms that Woroniecki continues to take his message "to the world and the streets of the nations". Woroniecki's current message, according to Wellman, taken from Woroniecki's website and literature, is: "Are you willing to look deeper, beyond it all? Please give us a chance to help you. It will be well worth your time. What we offer you is not a new group, "church", formulas, self exaltation or doctrines. What we offer you is the love of the living Jesus who has the answers that you are looking for". Woroniecki and his family currently run a 501(c)3 organization called Deep Heart, which is the organization they often tour under. Woroniecki remains active in his ministry and has recently visited various universities during the fall semesters. *O'Malley, Suzanne. Are You There Alone?: The Unspeakable Crime of Andrea Yates, Simon & Schuster, 2004. . *Spencer, Suzy. Breaking Point, St. Martin's Paperbacks, 2002. . *[http://www.michaelpeterworoniecki.com/ The Truth About Michael Woroniecki] - Joshua Woroniecki's blog *[http://www.michaelworoniecki.com/ michaelworoniecki.com] - The Official Website of the Woroniecki family Category:1954 births Category:Christian fundamentalism Category:Living people Category:Campus preachers Category:Central Michigan Chippewas football players Category:American football running backs Category:Aquinas College (Michigan) alumni Category:Former Roman Catholics Category:American Christian missionaries Category:American people of Polish descent Category:Fuller Theological Seminary alumni Category:People from Grand Rapids, Michigan

stable.title: Talk:42nd Street Shuttle
stable.raw_text: {{article history |action1=GAN |action1result=passed |action1date=9 November 2017 |action1link=Talk:42nd Street Shuttle/GA1 |action1oldid=809430268 |currentstatus=GA |topic=Transport |dykdate=21 November 2017 |dykentry= ... that trains on the '''[[42nd Street Shuttle]]''', the shortest route in the [[New York City Subway]] system, run a distance of {{convert|2700|ft|m}} in 90 seconds? }} {{WikiProject banner shell|class=GA| {{WikiProject Trains|importance=mid|NYPT=yes|NYPT-importance=high|subway=yes|portalSAweek=10, 2018}} {{WikiProject New York City|importance=low}} }} ==Requested move== S - Grand Central/Times Square Shuttle (New York City Subway service) → S - 42nd Street Shuttle (New York City Subway service) – This New York City Subway line is more commonly known as the "42nd Street Shuttle," rather than the lesser-used "Grand Central/Times Square Shuttle." [http://www.mta.info/nyct/service/sline.htm] This page was recently moved to the current name, and I moved it back, citing the reason just given. However, the same user inexplicably moved it again, without giving any reason for doing so. — [[User:Larry V|Larry V]] ([[User talk:Larry V|talk]]) 23:01, 19 June 2006 (UTC) ===Survey=== :''Add *'''Support''' or *'''Oppose''' followed by an optional one-sentence explanation, then sign your opinion with ''~~~~ *'''Support''' as per move request. — [[User:Larry V|Larry V]] ([[User talk:Larry V|talk]]) 23:01, 19 June 2006 (UTC) *'''Support'''. 42nd Street Shuttle is the common name and on Wikipedia we usually use the most common name as the article title. It is also the name the NYCTA uses on its timetable and web site[http://mta.info/nyct/service/subspick.htm] -- [[User:Cecropia|Cecropia]] 03:01, 20 June 2006 (UTC) *'''Strong Support'''; why was this moved anyway? [[User:Pacific Coast Highway|Pacific Coast Highway]] ([[User talk:Pacific Coast Highway|blah]] • [[Special:Contributions/Pacific Coast Highway|typa-typa]]) 03:09, 20 June 2006 (UTC) :*'''Comment''' I don't know, [[User:Imdanumber1|Imdanumber1]] initially moved it without providing any move summary, then did so again after I moved it back (providing explanation myself). — [[User:Larry V|Larry V]] ([[User talk:Larry V|talk]]) 03:46, 20 June 2006 (UTC) *'''Support'''; the MTA calls this line the 42nd Street Shuttle. So should Wikipedia. ===Discussion=== :''Add any additional comments'' What's the point of this whole discussion? Just do it. —The preceding [[Wikipedia:Signatures|unsigned]] comment was added by [[User:I.M. Rich|I.M. Rich]] ([[User talk:I.M. Rich|talk]] • [[Special:Contributions/I.M. Rich|contribs]]){{#if:{{{2|}}}| {{{2}}}|}}. ::Done -- [[User:Cecropia|Cecropia]] 14:54, 20 June 2006 (UTC) ===Moves and move protection=== I have tried to iron out these page titles. The current title [[S - 42nd Street Shuttle (New York City Subway service)]] is correct by style, usage and consensus. The former titles redirect here. I have also '''move protected''' this article so that anyone can edit it but only an admin can move it. If anyone attempts to move this again contrary to accuracy and consensus, he or she will be subject to blocks as a vandal, beginning with 24 hours. -- [[User:Cecropia|Cecropia]] 03:26, 28 June 2006 (UTC) :Excellent. --[[User:Larry V|Larry V]] ([[User talk:Larry V|talk]] | [[Special:Contributions/Larry V|contribs]]) 18:36, 28 June 2006 (UTC) ==Thing of the past== Maybe I should have discussed this first before I moved the page. Excuse me for the trouble. Besides, page discussions make things a lot easier. Look at the A-C (New York City Subway Service). The split of the article was my idea, and so I think page discussions save trouble. --[[User:imdanumber1|imdanumber1]] 15:21, 8 August 2006 (UTC) ==Article name== The current name of this article was chosen before I became heavily involved in the subway project, so I am not fully briefed on the history. The issue was apparently contentious enough that, at some point in the past, the article was move-protected (and still is). The very sensible standard at [[WP:NAME]] says: :Generally, article naming should give priority to what the majority of English speakers would most easily recognize, with a reasonable minimum of ambiguity, while at the same time making linking to those articles easy and second nature. :Another way to summarize the overall principle of Wikipedia's naming conventions: ::Names of Wikipedia articles should be optimized for ''readers'' over ''editors''; and for a ''general audience'' over ''specialists''. The same article says, "Use the most common name of a person or thing that does not conflict with the names of other people or things." The name of the present article seems to flunk that test, and badly. No one on earth would think of "S - 42nd Street Shuttle (New York City Subway service)" as the name of this article, unless that person were an expert on the [[WP:NYCS|WikiProject New York City Subway]]. It is an ugly name, and has far more qualifiers than necessary to disambiguate it from other names. Recently, [[Second Avenue Line]] was renamed [[Second Avenue Subway]] to conform with the usual guideline that things should be named for what the average person calls them. Why on earth isn't this article called, simply, [[42nd Street Shuttle]]? My question would apply with equal force to [[S - Rockaway Park Shuttle (New York City Subway service)]] and [[S - Franklin Avenue Shuttle (New York City Subway service)]], but I thought I'd ask it here first. [[User:Marc Shepherd|Marc Shepherd]] 14:05, 1 September 2006 (UTC) == Fire == Should there be something about the fire that occurred on the shuttle back then? All about the wooden platforms and the first truly automatic train which ran on electrical signals. I read about it on [http://www.nycsubway.org/lines/irtshuttle.html NYC Subway.org] [[User:Herenthere|Herenthere]] [[User talk:Herenthere|(Talk)]] 23:24, 5 February 2007 (UTC) == The "8" train == I am having difficulty finding a reliable source to support the claim that this line is known as "the 8" by Transit Operations? Does anyone have such a source? -[[User:Seidenstud|Seidenstud]] 14:13, 27 April 2007 (UTC) :Here are a few sources of varying reliability: *[http://books.google.com/books?id=X5TlBrYfMZoC&pg=PT276&lpg=PT276&dq=%22grand+central%22+shuttle+%228+train%22&source=web&ots=J9KVYBhvDe&sig=yVIEgAFkm3LskAFksXJ4z7lyt-k#PPT276,M1 "Many New Yorkers wonder why there is no such thing as an 8 train, and yet there is. The S, or Shuttle, train was once the 8—current lingo only for MTA employees in the know."] *[http://members.aol.com/bdmnqr2/linehistory.html] is written by a NYCTA worker, and says that "The Times Square Shuttle is currently designated 8 in RTO use." It's definitely not reliable though. :--[[User talk:NE2|NE2]] 18:47, 27 April 2007 (UTC) ::Eight was never a number for the 42nd Street Shuttle. Eight was the IRT Astoria Line, and then the 3rd Avenue Shuttle remnant in the Bronx. I can't say what RTO does, but in the schedule department, the internal letter or number for each route is on the PTT as the fifth character in a code beginning "tr00x." This letter or number is also in the URL for the PTT, e.g., "http://mta.info/nyct/service/pdf/tecur.pdf" for the E service. By this nomenclature, the only true "S" train is the Franklin Shuttle. The Rockaway Park Shuttle is "H" which was formally the official designation (or HH) for Rockaway Shuttles (earlier HH was the Court Street Shuttle). What is the 42nd Street Shuttle? Zero. Sorry, 42nd Street Shuttle. That's the breaks. -- [[User:Cecropia|Cecropia]] 15:02, 28 April 2007 (UTC) So what number is really used for 42nd Street shuttle by the NYCTA workers? 8?, or 0? It is confusing! Takuma Ishizeki[[User:Takuma Ishizeki|Takuma Ishizeki]] 22:01, 24 September 2007 (UTC) :I think it is a very minor point that has too much emphasis in the article, even if true. On top of that, we are not sure it is true. I would be happy to just see the statement deleted. [[User:Marc Shepherd|Marc Shepherd]] 23:13, 24 September 2007 (UTC) == Infobox troubles == I've mentioned this before, but I've been trying to reformat the infobox in a similar manner to the Franklin Avenue, and Rockaway Park shuttle infoboxes. Why have I been having so much trouble doing it? ----[[User:DanTD|DanTD]] ([[User talk:DanTD|talk]]) 14:46, 10 February 2008 (UTC) == Distance 0.8 mi? == The article claims the line is 0.8 mi long. That seems unlikely. Measuring it on Google Maps, I come up with anywhere from 2300 to 3300 feet. Google Maps doesn't have the exact track locations marked, so I just used various combinations of street exits to approximate. Somewhere in the range of 0.5 to 0.6 mi seems more likely. Is there some official MTA publication which says 0.8? -- [[User:RoySmith|RoySmith]] [[User Talk:RoySmith|(talk)]] 17:37, 27 August 2014 (UTC) {{Talk:42nd Street Shuttle/GA1}} == Who uses this? == Apologies if this is obvious, but who would take the trouble to use this, when it's only 0.4 miles long? Wouldn't it be quicker to walk between the two stations, esp. if you include the time it would take you to walk down the stairs and wait for the train? Thanks. '''[[User:Lugnuts|Lugnuts]]''' [[User talk:Lugnuts|Fire Walk with Me]] 20:54, 21 November 2017 (UTC) :It is by now way faster. It runs every 1 to 2 minutes, the stations aren't deep, and it is mainly used as a transfer between the 4, 5, and 6 and the 1, 2, 3, A, C, E, N, Q, R, and W. In fact, it has very heavy ridership during rush hours, which is why the MTA is considering removing seats from the cars on the trains. For the very few people that only travel between the two stations, it would still be quicker. It would take 5 to 6 minutes via the Shuttle, including walking, of which only 2 are spent on the shuttle. It would take ten or so minutes to walk. If this isn't clear, please respond. Thanks for the question.--[[User:Kew Gardens 613|Kew Gardens 613]] ([[User talk:Kew Gardens 613|talk]]) 01:01, 22 November 2017 (UTC) ::Thanks - that's useful. '''[[User:Lugnuts|Lugnuts]]''' [[User talk:Lugnuts|Fire Walk with Me]] 07:49, 22 November 2017 (UTC) == Historical Images == {{Re|Epicgenius}} The MTA put [https://www.flickr.com/photos/mtaphotos/48432565816/in/album-72157710033787012/ three historical pictures of the shuttle] up on Flickr, with the license being all rights reserved. I am just making sure, but is it okay to upload these to Commons? Thanks.--[[User:Kew Gardens 613|Kew Gardens 613]] ([[User talk:Kew Gardens 613|talk]]) 03:00, 19 August 2019 (UTC) :{{u|Kew Gardens 613}}, yes, it fits the requisite licensing criteria (CC-BY 2.0). There's a tool called [[:c:Commons:Flickr2Commons|Commons:Flickr2Commons]] that you can use to transfer the files over yourself. [[User:Epicgenius|epicgenius]] ([[User talk:Epicgenius|talk]]) 03:05, 19 August 2019 (UTC) == Track 3 is now closed due to upgrade construction == Track 3 will eventually be taken out of service as Track 1 and Track 4 get longer platforms at three Grand Central Station and Times Square Station. For now, track 3 is not being used. Shouldn't this be reflected in this article? [[user:mnw2000]] 20:05, 25 September 2019 (UTC) == Shuttle is now closed due to lack of crews, and crews being reassigned to other lines == As of March 2020, due to lack of ridership and train crew availability caused by the [[2019–20 coronavirus pandemic]], the '''S''' is temporarily suspended, with the '''7''' providing alternate service.{{Cite web|url=https://new.mta.info/precautions-against-coronavirus|title=MTA Essential Service During the Coronavirus Pandemic|website=MTA|language=en|access-date=2020-03-26}} Yep - y'all left this one out. {{reflist-talk}} * Per [[WP:NOTNEWS]], we generally don't include this. However, I see it is already included in the Operations section. [[User:Epicgenius|epicgenius]] ([[User talk:Epicgenius|talk]]) 13:31, 1 May 2020 (UTC) == Featured Article == {{Re|Epicgenius}} I eventually want to nominate this for Featured Article Status, maybe over the summer. I can see a few issues that would need to be resolved before I nominate it, including the use of Joseph Brennan's site, and questionable sources for ad-wraps and movie filming. I also feel as though more research would need to be done on the construction of the original subway in this section, and the Mineola spur for August Belmont. I will also need to add the information from my early 1990s-report about the plan to rebuild Times Square, including an older version of the plan to rebuild the shuttle. Some background on the history of the IRT, and why it was designed as a Z, with a section on 42nd Street should be added as well. Information on the type of subway construction, and the dimensions of the tunneling should be added. Also, information on how construction of the H-system was done should be added, if possible. More research is needed on the 1920s and 1930s plan to create a loop using the shuttle, a new track at 33rd Street station, along 31st Street, up Seventh Avenue and back onto the shuttle. Information on the line's historic signaling would be great, but it would be hard to find. More information on why the 1949 and 1954 projects were never completed should be found. Information on the line's rolling stock should be added as well. More information on the 1964 fire, including suspicions of sabotage should be included. Information on the delays in the project in the early 2000s should also be added. Are there any other things that you think would need to be done before nominating the article? Thanks.--[[User:Kew Gardens 613|Kew Gardens 613]] ([[User talk:Kew Gardens 613|talk]]) 10:59, 1 May 2020 (UTC) Come to think of it, a better source for its being internally known as the 0 should be added.--[[User:Kew Gardens 613|Kew Gardens 613]] ([[User talk:Kew Gardens 613|talk]]) 11:01, 1 May 2020 (UTC) :{{u|Kew Gardens 613}}, you pointed out the right issues. But besides that, I would ask for a copy-edit, for instance from the [[WP:GOCE|Guild of Copy Editors]]. Grammar-wise, there are a few duplicate links, several abbreviations (like IRT) that might have to be cut down, and a couple of jargon terms. Finally, you have to make sure that, although you are adding relevant info, that the history isn't too long and doesn't delve into too much granular detail. [[User:Epicgenius|epicgenius]] ([[User talk:Epicgenius|talk]]) 13:30, 1 May 2020 (UTC) ::{{Re|Epicgenius}} Thanks for the suggestions. In terms of the history, I think that there might be enough for a [[History of the 42nd Street Shuttle]] article. Do you agree?--[[User:Kew Gardens 613|Kew Gardens 613]] ([[User talk:Kew Gardens 613|talk]]) 13:35, 1 May 2020 (UTC) :::{{u|Kew Gardens 613}}, there might be enough info for such an article. We'd also have to figure out where the history of the shuttle fits in, relative to the [[early history of the IRT subway]]. [[User:Epicgenius|epicgenius]] ([[User talk:Epicgenius|talk]]) 13:37, 1 May 2020 (UTC) == Full Shutdown of 42nd Street Shuttle == {{Re|Epicgenius}} Apparently, the shuttle is out for the whole year. I am not sure how best to describe this in the lead. Other articles might need to be updated. Also, I think the chronology of the article might need to be reworked. The information on the temporary closure of the shuttle is in the operations section. Thanks. --[[User:Kew Gardens 613|Kew Gardens 613]] ([[User talk:Kew Gardens 613|talk]]) 10:57, 11 May 2020 (UTC) :{{u|Kew Gardens 613}}, that is good to know. In the lead and history sections, it says the shuttle is out due to the reconstruction, but in the body it says the shuttle is out due to COVID. I think this needs to be fixed - it was initially suspended due to COVID but now has been extended to last the entire year for reconstruction. The only articles that need to be updated (if I remember correctly) are the Grand Central and Times Square pages, and the respective service templates. [[User:Epicgenius|epicgenius]] ([[User talk:Epicgenius|talk]]) 13:25, 11 May 2020 (UTC) == Tracks layout == According to YouTuber/Railfan DJ Hammer, as of November 7th, 2020, Track 3 of 42nd Street Shuttle has officially abandoned. I want someone to redesign the layout map with the Track 3 abandoned. Please check {https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9qyuANBC1a4} for more information. Thanks. [[User:JC1199154]] * Update: Thank you [[User:Kew Gardens 613|Kew Gardens 613]] for making changes on the track layout. I deeply appreciate the change. — Preceding [[Wikipedia:Signatures|unsigned]] comment added by [[User:JC1199154|JC1199154]] ([[User talk:JC1199154#top|talk]] • [[Special:Contributions/JC1199154|contribs]]) 04:35, 12 November 2020 (UTC) :{{ping|Kew Gardens 613}} {{ping|Epicgenius}} Could we do something similar to the "Station reconstruction track diagrams" at [[Wilson station (CTA)]] to show how the shuttle layout has changed over the last century? '''[[User:Cards84664|Cards]][[User talk:Cards84664|84664]]''' 16:20, 16 November 2020 (UTC) ::{{u|Cards84664}}, sure. I can do that. [[User:Epicgenius|epicgenius]] ([[User talk:Epicgenius|talk]]) 16:21, 16 November 2020 (UTC) == Route map and good article "+" == Currently at least on my screen the Good Article "+" sign covers up the dropdown arrow to display the route map in the upper right corner of the screen. Anyone know how to fix this? [[User:CapitalSasha|''C''apital''S''asha]] ~ [[User talk:CapitalSasha|''t''alk]] 20:32, 12 April 2022 (UTC) == R62A car assignments == In the section from 1980 to 2000s, I put back my statement of the 3-4-3 car assignments at the Times Square station. It was stated by Epicgenius that a reliable source was needed. I used the shuttle DAILY when going to/from work. That would qualify me as a reliable source.[[User:IRT1904|Allan]] ([[User talk:IRT1904|talk]]) 18:47, 25 October 2023 (UTC) :That's not what a [[WP:RS|reliable source]] is. [[User:Doniago|DonIago]] ([[User talk:Doniago|talk]]) 19:11, 25 October 2023 (UTC) ::Agreed. Reliable sources are required to have been, at the very minimum, published. According to [[WP:PUBLISHED]], media can qualify as reliable sources that {{tq|Like text, media must be produced by a reliable source and be properly cited. Additionally, an archived copy of the media must exist.}} As such, a personal observation does not qualify as a reliable source. If Wikipedia accepted personal observations as sources, then anyone could potentially make up an observation to add to Wikipedia.{{pb}}Additionally, the 3-4-3 car assignments are already in the article.The first paragraph of the Operation section says, "Prior to the shuttle's renovation project, shuttle service was provided by three-car trains on tracks 1 and 4, and a four-car train on track 3", citing [https://web.archive.org/web/20190819025539/http://cbsix.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Times-Square-ADA_MN-CB5_final.pdf this source] (see page 17). This is a slideshow of a presentation, and there may well be better sources available, but it ''is'' cited already. – [[User:Epicgenius|Epicgenius]] ([[User talk:Epicgenius|talk]]) 22:44, 25 October 2023 (UTC) == third track removal == The text states "With the removal of track 3, it remains physically impossible for a train to go from the IRT Lexington Avenue Line to the IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line or vice versa by using the shuttle tracks.", but from the diagrams on this page for the 3-track system, it doesn't seem possible to get a track from the Lex to 7th lines even with the third track there. Am I missing something? [[User:Tduk|Tduk]] ([[User talk:Tduk|talk]]) 15:35, 7 January 2024 (UTC) :{{re|Tduk}} Perhaps this should say "Even with the removal of track 3..."? I understand this to mean that trains could cross between the lines via the shuttle tracks neither before nor after the 2020 renovation. Maybe an early proposal or draft for the renovation included construction of a crossover, though a quick search isn't turning up any sources supporting this idea. [[User:ComplexRational|'''Complex''']]/[[User talk:ComplexRational|'''Rational''']] 22:06, 16 January 2024 (UTC) ::::I see what you're saying, it is oddly phrased. It really doesn't need to be there at all. [[User:Tduk|Tduk]] ([[User talk:Tduk|talk]]) 22:22, 16 January 2024 (UTC) == In film section? == Don’t know if it would warrant it or not but one of the key scenes from the 1971 film The French Connection takes place on the Grand Central shuttle platform where Gene Hackman’s Popeye Doyle plays a game of cat and mouse with Fernando Rey’s Alain Charnier. I’m not sure if other films or TV shows were shot on the platform but given the amount of things that have used the terminal and subway in and around GCT I have to imagine there were more. — Preceding [[Wikipedia:Signatures|unsigned]] comment added by [[Special:Contributions/~2026-57479|~2026-57479]] ([[User talk:~2026-57479#top|talk]]) :If it's just a listing of times when the shuttle appears in film then I'd be less inclined to be in favor of it than if some additional information regarding it could be provided. In any event, [[WP:SECONDARY|secondary]] sources would be needed to build-out such a section as per [[WP:IPCV]]; what we absolutely shouldn't have is a barebones listing along the lines of, "The shuttle also appears in X.". [[User:Doniago|DonIago]] ([[User talk:Doniago|talk]]) 03:59, 5 January 2026 (UTC)

Measuring it on Google Maps, I come up with anywhere from 2300 to 3300 feet. Google Maps doesn't have the exact track locations marked, so I just used various combinations of street exits to approximate. Somewhere in the range of 0.5 to 0.6 mi seems more likely. Is there some official MTA publication which says 0.8? -- RoySmith (talk) 17:37, 27 August 2014 (UTC) Apologies if this is obvious, but who would take the trouble to use this, when it's only 0.4 miles long? Wouldn't it be quicker to walk between the two stations, esp. if you include the time it would take you to walk down the stairs and wait for the train? Thanks. Lugnuts Fire Walk with Me 20:54, 21 November 2017 (UTC) :It is by now way faster. It runs every 1 to 2 minutes, the stations aren't deep, and it is mainly used as a transfer between the 4, 5, and 6 and the 1, 2, 3, A, C, E, N, Q, R, and W. In fact, it has very heavy ridership during rush hours, which is why the MTA is considering removing seats from the cars on the trains. For the very few people that only travel between the two stations, it would still be quicker. It would take 5 to 6 minutes via the Shuttle, including walking, of which only 2 are spent on the shuttle. It would take ten or so minutes to walk. If this isn't clear, please respond. Thanks for the question.--Kew Gardens 613 (talk) 01:01, 22 November 2017 (UTC) ::Thanks - that's useful. Lugnuts Fire Walk with Me 07:49, 22 November 2017 (UTC) The MTA put [https://www.flickr.com/photos/mtaphotos/48432565816/in/album-72157710033787012/ three historical pictures of the shuttle] up on Flickr, with the license being all rights reserved. I am just making sure, but is it okay to upload these to Commons? Thanks.--Kew Gardens 613 (talk) 03:00, 19 August 2019 (UTC) :, yes, it fits the requisite licensing criteria (CC-BY 2.0). There's a tool called Commons:Flickr2Commons that you can use to transfer the files over yourself. epicgenius (talk) 03:05, 19 August 2019 (UTC) Track 3 will eventually be taken out of service as Track 1 and Track 4 get longer platforms at three Grand Central Station and Times Square Station. For now, track 3 is not being used. Shouldn't this be reflected in this article? user:mnw2000 20:05, 25 September 2019 (UTC) As of March 2020, due to lack of ridership and train crew availability caused by the 2019–20 coronavirus pandemic, the S is temporarily suspended, with the 7 providing alternate service. Yep - y'all left this one out. * Per WP:NOTNEWS, we generally don't include this. However, I see it is already included in the Operations section. epicgenius (talk) 13:31, 1 May 2020 (UTC) I eventually want to nominate this for Featured Article Status, maybe over the summer. I can see a few issues that would need to be resolved before I nominate it, including the use of Joseph Brennan's site, and questionable sources for ad-wraps and movie filming. I also feel as though more research would need to be done on the construction of the original subway in this section, and the Mineola spur for August Belmont. I will also need to add the information from my early 1990s-report about the plan to rebuild Times Square, including an older version of the plan to rebuild the shuttle. Some background on the history of the IRT, and why it was designed as a Z, with a section on 42nd Street should be added as well. Information on the type of subway construction, and the dimensions of the tunneling should be added. Also, information on how construction of the H-system was done should be added, if possible. More research is needed on the 1920s and 1930s plan to create a loop using the shuttle, a new track at 33rd Street station, along 31st Street, up Seventh Avenue and back onto the shuttle. Information on the line's historic signaling would be great, but it would be hard to find. More information on why the 1949 and 1954 projects were never completed should be found. Information on the line's rolling stock should be added as well. More information on the 1964 fire, including suspicions of sabotage should be included. Information on the delays in the project in the early 2000s should also be added. Are there any other things that you think would need to be done before nominating the article? Thanks.--Kew Gardens 613 (talk) 10:59, 1 May 2020 (UTC) Come to think of it, a better source for its being internally known as the 0 should be added.--Kew Gardens 613 (talk) 11:01, 1 May 2020 (UTC) :, you pointed out the right issues. But besides that, I would ask for a copy-edit, for instance from the Guild of Copy Editors. Grammar-wise, there are a few duplicate links, several abbreviations (like IRT) that might have to be cut down, and a couple of jargon terms. Finally, you have to make sure that, although you are adding relevant info, that the history isn't too long and doesn't delve into too much granular detail. epicgenius (talk) 13:30, 1 May 2020 (UTC) :: Thanks for the suggestions. In terms of the history, I think that there might be enough for a History of the 42nd Street Shuttle article. Do you agree?--Kew Gardens 613 (talk) 13:35, 1 May 2020 (UTC) :::, there might be enough info for such an article. We'd also have to figure out where the history of the shuttle fits in, relative to the early history of the IRT subway. epicgenius (talk) 13:37, 1 May 2020 (UTC) Apparently, the shuttle is out for the whole year. I am not sure how best to describe this in the lead. Other articles might need to be updated. Also, I think the chronology of the article might need to be reworked. The information on the temporary closure of the shuttle is in the operations section. Thanks. --Kew Gardens 613 (talk) 10:57, 11 May 2020 (UTC) :, that is good to know. In the lead and history sections, it says the shuttle is out due to the reconstruction, but in the body it says the shuttle is out due to COVID. I think this needs to be fixed - it was initially suspended due to COVID but now has been extended to last the entire year for reconstruction. The only articles that need to be updated (if I remember correctly) are the Grand Central and Times Square pages, and the respective service templates. epicgenius (talk) 13:25, 11 May 2020 (UTC) According to YouTuber/Railfan DJ Hammer, as of November 7th, 2020, Track 3 of 42nd Street Shuttle has officially abandoned. I want someone to redesign the layout map with the Track 3 abandoned. Please check {https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9qyuANBC1a4} for more information. Thanks. User:JC1199154 * Update: Thank you Kew Gardens 613 for making changes on the track layout. I deeply appreciate the change. — Preceding unsigned comment added by JC1199154 (talk • contribs) 04:35, 12 November 2020 (UTC) : Could we do something similar to the "Station reconstruction track diagrams" at Wilson station (CTA) to show how the shuttle layout has changed over the last century? Cards84664 16:20, 16 November 2020 (UTC) ::, sure. I can do that. epicgenius (talk) 16:21, 16 November 2020 (UTC) Currently at least on my screen the Good Article "+" sign covers up the dropdown arrow to display the route map in the upper right corner of the screen. Anyone know how to fix this? CapitalSasha ~ talk 20:32, 12 April 2022 (UTC) In the section from 1980 to 2000s, I put back my statement of the 3-4-3 car assignments at the Times Square station. It was stated by Epicgenius that a reliable source was needed. I used the shuttle DAILY when going to/from work. That would qualify me as a reliable source.Allan (talk) 18:47, 25 October 2023 (UTC) :That's not what a reliable source is. DonIago (talk) 19:11, 25 October 2023 (UTC) ::Agreed. Reliable sources are required to have been, at the very minimum, published. According to WP:PUBLISHED, media can qualify as reliable sources that As such, a personal observation does not qualify as a reliable source. If Wikipedia accepted personal observations as sources, then anyone could potentially make up an observation to add to Wikipedia.Additionally, the 3-4-3 car assignments are already in the article.The first paragraph of the Operation section says, "Prior to the shuttle's renovation project, shuttle service was provided by three-car trains on tracks 1 and 4, and a four-car train on track 3", citing [https://web.archive.org/web/20190819025539/http://cbsix.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/06/Times-Square-ADA_MN-CB5_final.pdf this source] (see page 17). This is a slideshow of a presentation, and there may well be better sources available, but it is cited already. – Epicgenius (talk) 22:44, 25 October 2023 (UTC) The text states "With the removal of track 3, it remains physically impossible for a train to go from the IRT Lexington Avenue Line to the IRT Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line or vice versa by using the shuttle tracks.", but from the diagrams on this page for the 3-track system, it doesn't seem possible to get a track from the Lex to 7th lines even with the third track there. Am I missing something? Tduk (talk) 15:35, 7 January 2024 (UTC) : Perhaps this should say "Even with the removal of track 3..."? I understand this to mean that trains could cross between the lines via the shuttle tracks neither before nor after the 2020 renovation. Maybe an early proposal or draft for the renovation included construction of a crossover, though a quick search isn't turning up any sources supporting this idea. Complex/Rational 22:06, 16 January 2024 (UTC) ::::I see what you're saying, it is oddly phrased. It really doesn't need to be there at all. Tduk (talk) 22:22, 16 January 2024 (UTC) Don’t know if it would warrant it or not but one of the key scenes from the 1971 film The French Connection takes place on the Grand Central shuttle platform where Gene Hackman’s Popeye Doyle plays a game of cat and mouse with Fernando Rey’s Alain Charnier. I’m not sure if other films or TV shows were shot on the platform but given the amount of things that have used the terminal and subway in and around GCT I have to imagine there were more. — Preceding unsigned comment added by ~2026-57479 (talk) :If it's just a listing of times when the shuttle appears in film then I'd be less inclined to be in favor of it than if some additional information regarding it could be provided. In any event, secondary sources would be needed to build-out such a section as per WP:IPCV; what we absolutely shouldn't have is a barebones listing along the lines of, "The shuttle also appears in X.".
